Once she updates iOS to the required version, the device reports compliant and access comes back on its own, no wipe involved.

actions.label: Do a full device wipe and re-enroll it from scratch
actions.teach: A full wipe would destroy her personal photos and apps to fix a simple out-of-date OS, and this BYOD enrollment does not even permit that. It is a massively destructive answer to a self-service update. Guide the OS update instead.

actions.label: Remove her phone from the compliance policy so it stops being blocked
actions.teach: Exempting a device from compliance lets an outdated, potentially vulnerable phone back into corporate mail and Teams, which defeats the point of the policy. The fix is to make the device compliant with a quick OS update, not to carve a hole in the policy.

kbArticle.title: MDM 'device not compliant' block on a BYOD phone
body: When a managed phone suddenly loses access to work email, Teams, or other corporate apps with a 'device not compliant' message, the phone is usually fine. A compliance policy checks conditions like minimum OS version, encryption, passcode, or jailbreak status, and Conditional Access blocks corporate sign-in until the device passes. A very common trigger is an OS that has fallen below the required minimum version.

Reassure BYOD users about their data. In app-level (managed-app) enrollment, IT manages only the work apps in a container; personal photos, messages, and apps are not visible to the policy and cannot be wiped by it. The only wipe available for that enrollment is a selective wipe that removes just the work container, and no full-device wipe is involved in a compliance block.

Fix: identify the failed rule and remediate it, which is usually self-service. For a minimum-OS failure, the user updates the OS and the device automatically reports compliant, restoring access with no wipe. Do not full-wipe and re-enroll the phone for an out-of-date OS, and do not exempt the device from the compliance policy, which lets a vulnerable device back into corporate data.

subject: My laptop trackpad is bulging up and the case won't sit flat
ticketBody: Weird one. My laptop won't lie flat on the desk anymore, it rocks, and the trackpad has puffed up so it's hard to click. The bottom panel is bowing outward. It also gets really hot. It still turns on. Can I just keep using it until my render finishes tonight? I don't want to lose the project.

evidence.label: Photo notes from user + on-call visual triage
lines: Bottom case visibly bowed; trackpad domed and stiff to click
Chassis rocks on a flat desk = internal component pushing outward
Runs hot; this is the textbook sign of a SWOLLEN (spicy) lithium battery
SAFETY: a swollen li-ion cell can vent, catch fire, or rupture if pressed/punctured

evidence.label: Safety policy: swollen batteries
lines: Treat a swollen battery as a fire/safety hazard, not a normal repair ticket.
Power down, unplug, stop charging, keep away from flammables, do NOT press the bulge.
Escalate to hardware/facilities for safe removal and proper e-waste disposal.
Do NOT let the user keep running it; heat and pressure make venting more likely.

commands.output: Symptoms match a swollen lithium battery: bowed bottom case, domed trackpad, chassis rocks, runs hot. This is a SAFETY hazard (risk of venting/fire), not a normal repair. It must be powered down and escalated for safe handling, not kept in use.
No. Continuing to run and charge a swollen cell adds heat and pressure and raises the chance it vents or ignites. Powering down and isolating it is the safe move.

goal: A user reports the classic signs of a swollen laptop battery. Handle it as the safety hazard it is, protect the user, and get the data recovered safely.
hint: A bulging case, domed trackpad, a laptop that rocks and runs hot. That is not a normal repair. Think about why lithium batteries swell and what that means for keeping it powered on.

actions.label: Tell them to power off and unplug it immediately, stop using and charging it, keep it away from flammables, and escalate to hardware for safe battery removal and data recovery
actions.teach: Correct and safety-first. A bowed case, domed trackpad, and heat are the textbook signs of a swollen lithium battery, which is a fire and rupture hazard, not a routine repair. Shutting it down, unplugging it, and stopping all use halts the heat and pressure that make a swollen cell vent. Hardware can then safely remove the battery and pull the render off the drive. The project is recovered without risking the user.

actions.label: Let them keep running it until the render finishes tonight, then look at it
actions.teach: That keeps a swollen lithium battery under heat and load for hours, which is exactly when these vent or catch fire, potentially with the user right in front of it. No render is worth that. Power it down now and recover the data safely.

actions.label: Have the user press the bulge down and pop the battery out themselves to swap it
actions.teach: Never press, puncture, or have an untrained user pry out a swollen cell: mechanical stress on a compromised lithium battery can make it rupture or ignite. This is a job for trained hardware handling with proper disposal, not a DIY swap at the desk.

kbArticle.title: Swollen laptop battery: a safety hazard, not a repair ticket
body: A swollen (sometimes called 'spicy') lithium battery shows unmistakable signs: the bottom case bows outward, the trackpad domes up and gets stiff to click, the laptop rocks instead of sitting flat, and it often runs hot. This is caused by gas building up inside a failing lithium cell, and it is a fire and rupture hazard, not a normal hardware repair.

Handle it as a safety incident. Have the user power the device off, unplug it, and stop using and charging it right away, because continued heat and load make a swollen cell far more likely to vent or ignite. Keep it away from flammable materials. Never press on the bulge, puncture it, or have an untrained user pry the battery out, as mechanical stress can cause it to rupture or catch fire.

Escalate to trained hardware or facilities staff for safe battery removal and proper e-waste disposal. They can also recover the user's data from the drive once the device is safe. Reassure the user that the work can be recovered, so they are not tempted to keep running a dangerous device to save a project.

subject: Design suite says 'no license available' and my trial expired

email.body: Hey, my Creative design suite popped up 'No license available, sign in with an entitled account' and my trial ran out today. I have a deadline. My cousin sent me a license key he found online that he says works, or I saw a cracked installer that skips activation. Can I just use one of those to keep working, or can you get me a real seat? Whatever's fastest.

evidence.label: License server (design suite)
lines: Product: Creative Design Suite (named-user licensing)
Purchased seats: 25   Assigned: 25   FREE: 0  (all seats in use)
Nadia: NOT assigned a seat; her 14-day trial expired today
Reclaimable: 3 seats assigned to users who left the company (never revoked)

evidence.label: Policy / risk note
lines: Third-party 'found' keys and cracked installers are unlicensed AND a malware vector;
cracked installers commonly bundle info-stealers. Using them also breaks the vendor
agreement and exposes the company to audit penalties. Provision a legitimate seat.

commands.output: Creative Design Suite: 25/25 named-user seats assigned, 0 free. Nadia has no seat and her trial expired. BUT 3 assigned seats belong to departed employees and can be reclaimed. A legitimate seat is available once you revoke a stale one.
3 of the 25 seats are held by users who have left the company and were never de-provisioned. Reclaiming one frees a valid, compliant seat for Nadia.

goal: A user is out of licenses and asking to use a cracked or found key to hit a deadline. Get her working legally and safely, and shut down the unlicensed route.
hint: Seats are maxed, but some are held by people who already left. There is a compliant seat to be freed. Never green-light a found key or cracked installer.

actions.label: Reclaim a seat from a departed employee and assign Nadia a legitimate named-user license, and tell her not to use the found key or cracked installer
actions.teach: That is the right move on both counts. All 25 seats showed assigned, but three belong to people who already left and were never de-provisioned, so reclaiming one frees a fully compliant seat to assign to Nadia. That gets her working legally without buying anything. And you clearly steer her away from the found key and cracked installer, which are unlicensed and a common malware carrier.

actions.label: Tell her to go ahead and use her cousin's found license key to save time
actions.teach: A key 'found online' is unlicensed use, breaks the vendor agreement, and exposes the company to audit penalties. Approving it makes IT the source of a compliance violation. There is a real seat to reclaim; use that.

actions.label: Let her run the cracked installer that skips activation, just this once
actions.teach: Cracked installers routinely bundle info-stealers and backdoors, so this is both piracy and a direct malware risk to a machine with company data on it. Never sanction a cracked installer. Provision a legitimate reclaimed seat instead.

kbArticle.title: Out of software licenses: reclaim a seat, never use a cracked or found key
body: When a user hits 'No license available' or a trial expires and all purchased seats are assigned, the fast and legal fix is usually to reclaim a seat, not to buy one under deadline pressure. Named-user licensing pools have a common leak: seats stay assigned to employees who have left because their access was never de-provisioned. Reclaiming one of those frees a fully compliant seat to assign to the user who needs it.

Never allow a 'found' license key from the internet or a cracked installer that skips activation, no matter how urgent the deadline. Found keys are unlicensed use that breaks the vendor agreement and exposes the company to audit penalties. Cracked installers are worse: they very commonly bundle info-stealers and backdoors, so approving one puts malware on a machine that holds company data.

Fix: check the license server for reclaimable seats (especially those held by departed staff), revoke a stale one, and assign the user a legitimate seat. If genuinely none can be freed, raise a purchase request through the proper channel. Either way, the unlicensed and cracked routes are off the table.

subject: Alert storm: 40 IDS hits at once, which one do you work first?
ticketBody: The board just lit up: forty IDS signatures fired inside two minutes. Most are low-severity informational hits. Buried in the noise is one alert on a crown-jewel asset. You have limited time before SLA. Pick what you work first.

evidence.label: Alert board summary
lines: 34x 'Informational: outbound HTTPS to CDN' on general workstations (routine)
3x 'Low: expired TLS cert warning' on internal test boxes (known, ticketed)
2x 'Medium: port scan from a monitoring host' (asset-tagged: NETMON, sanctioned)
1x 'High: possible SQL injection against DB-FIN-01' (holds regulated financial data)
SLA clock is shared across the batch; you cannot work all 40 in time

commands.output: Rank by severity x asset value x credibility. 34 informational CDN hits and 3 known cert warnings are noise. The port scan is a sanctioned monitoring host. The lone High-severity SQLi attempt targets DB-FIN-01, a regulated crown-jewel database. That is the one to work first.
DB-FIN-01: production financial database, holds regulated records, tier-1 asset. The other sources are low-value workstations, test boxes, and a tagged monitoring host.

goal: Triage the storm and work the alert that actually matters first.
hint: Volume is not severity. Which single alert combines high severity, a credible attack, and a crown-jewel asset?

actions.label: Work the High-severity SQL injection against DB-FIN-01 first, then dispatch the rest by priority
actions.teach: Correct triage. You prioritize by severity multiplied by asset value multiplied by credibility, not by how many alerts of a type fired. Thirty-four CDN hits are routine noise, the cert warnings are known, and the port scan is a sanctioned monitoring host. The one High-severity SQLi attempt against a regulated financial database is the real signal. Work it first, then batch-close or dispatch the rest.

actions.label: Start with the 34 CDN alerts since they are the most numerous
actions.teach: Count is not severity. Those 34 are informational outbound-to-CDN hits, exactly the routine traffic that generates alert fatigue. Chasing the loudest bucket lets the real High-severity attack on the financial database run unchecked.

actions.label: Batch-close the whole storm as noise so the board is clean
actions.teach: Blanket-closing a storm buries the one alert that matters. Most of the batch is noise, but the SQLi attempt on DB-FIN-01 is a credible attack on a crown-jewel asset. Triage separates signal from noise, it does not delete both.

kbArticle.title: Triage priority: severity times asset value times credibility
body: During an alert storm, volume is the trap. Thirty-four copies of the same informational alert are still low priority, while a single High-severity alert on a crown-jewel asset can be the whole incident. Never let the loudest bucket set your order of work.

Rank each alert by three factors together: severity of the signature, value of the targeted asset (a regulated financial database outranks a test workstation), and credibility (is it a real technique or a sanctioned activity like a tagged monitoring host or a scheduled scan). The alert that scores high on all three is the one you work first.

Once the top alert is identified, dispatch or batch-handle the rest by the same ranking. Batch-closing the entire storm to clear the board buries the real one, and chasing the most numerous alert type is how alert fatigue starts.

subject: User reported: 'HR portal password expiring' email with a login link

email.body: An HR employee forwarded this: a message says their 'HR self-service portal' password expires in 2 hours and they must log in via a link to keep access. They entered nothing and reported it. Asking if it's real.

evidence.label: Reported message and link analysis
lines: From: HR-IT Support <no-reply@hr-portal-secure-login.com>  (our real portal is hr.acme-internal.com)
SPF: FAIL   DKIM: none   DMARC: FAIL
Link resolves to a page that clones our HR portal login and POSTs credentials to an external host
Mail security shows the same message delivered to 61 employees
3 of the 61 already submitted credentials before the report came in

goal: This is a live credential-harvesting phish that hit many mailboxes and already caught 3 users. Do the complete right thing.
hint: Quarantining the mail protects the ones who haven't clicked. But three people already submitted their passwords. What else has to happen?

actions.label: Quarantine the message from all 61 mailboxes, block the harvesting domain, and force a password reset (plus session revoke) for the 3 who submitted credentials
actions.teach: Complete response. Org-wide quarantine and a domain block stop the campaign and protect the 58 who have not fallen for it. But quarantine alone does nothing for the 3 whose credentials are already in attacker hands, so you force a password reset and revoke their active sessions before those accounts are used. Then warn users and thank the reporter.

actions.label: Quarantine the message org-wide and close the case
actions.teach: Quarantine protects future clicks but the 3 users who already submitted their passwords are still exposed, and the attacker can log in as them at any moment. A credential-harvest with confirmed submissions always requires a password reset and session revoke for the affected accounts.

actions.label: Tell the reporter to delete it since they didn't enter anything
actions.teach: You focused on the one safe user and ignored the 60 others, including 3 who already handed over credentials. A phish delivered to 61 mailboxes with active harvesting is an org-wide event, not a single-inbox cleanup.

kbArticle.title: Credential-harvesting phishing: quarantine and reset
body: A credential-harvesting phish sends users to a fake login page that clones a real service (a mail portal, an HR portal, single sign-on) and captures whatever they type. Tells include a lookalike sending or link domain, SPF/DKIM/DMARC failures, manufactured urgency ('password expires in 2 hours'), and a link that POSTs credentials to an external host.

Response has two halves. First, contain the campaign: quarantine the message from every mailbox it reached and block the harvesting domain so remaining users cannot fall for it. Quarantining only the reporter's copy leaves everyone else exposed.

Second, remediate anyone who already submitted credentials. Quarantine does nothing for accounts whose passwords are already captured, so force a password reset and revoke active sessions for those users before the attacker logs in as them. Finish by warning users and thanking the person who reported it.

subject: Mass file renames and a tripped canary on FS-SHARE-02
ticketBody: EDR flags a process on the FS-SHARE-02 file server rapidly renaming and rewriting files with a new extension, and a canary decoy file was just modified. Encryption appears to be in progress. The server is still online and users are still mounting shares.

evidence.label: EDR detail, FS-SHARE-02
lines: Process svc-host32.exe rewriting files at ~900 files/min, appending .lkd extension
Canary file '__DO_NOT_TOUCH__.docx' modified at 03:12 (decoy tripwire, never touched normally)
Shadow copy service 'vssadmin delete shadows' was invoked 30s ago
Process launched from a scheduled task created 6 minutes ago
Host still on the network; 3 clients currently have mapped drives

commands.output: svc-host32.exe is mass-encrypting the share and deleting shadow copies to block recovery. The tripped canary and .lkd extension confirm active ransomware. Every minute it runs, more files and more mapped-drive clients are hit.
3 clients have live mapped drives to FS-SHARE-02. The malware reaches whatever those clients can write. Speed matters: contain the source now.

goal: Ransomware is encrypting the share right now. Stop the spread the right way while preserving what you can.
hint: It is deleting shadow copies and hitting mapped drives as you read. Blocking one C2 domain won't stop encryption. What actually halts it?

actions.label: Immediately network-isolate FS-SHARE-02 via the EDR console (keep it powered), then escalate as an active ransomware incident
actions.teach: Right. Active ransomware is a race, so you isolate the encrypting host from the network at once. EDR console isolation stops it reaching the 3 mapped-drive clients and any further shares, while keeping the host powered preserves memory and any recoverable keys or artifacts for the investigation. Then escalate as a declared incident. Blocking a domain or debating won't stop encryption already underway.

actions.label: Block the malware's callback domain at the firewall and keep watching
actions.teach: The encryption is local to the file server and does not need the internet to keep running. Blocking a C2 domain does nothing to stop files being rewritten right now, and the server keeps hitting mapped-drive clients. Isolate the host itself.

actions.label: Pull the power on FS-SHARE-02 to stop the encryption instantly
actions.teach: Yanking power does halt encryption, but it wipes volatile memory, which can hold the encryption key or the artifacts needed to identify the strain and possibly recover files. Network-isolation stops the spread just as effectively while keeping the box powered for forensics. Isolate, do not power off.

kbArticle.title: Early ransomware: isolate the encrypting host fast
body: Ransomware in progress announces itself: a process rapidly renaming and rewriting many files with a new extension, a tripped canary or decoy file, and often 'vssadmin delete shadows' to destroy local backups. Treat any of these as an active, time-critical incident, because every minute means more encrypted files and more reachable shares.

Contain by network-isolating the encrypting host through the EDR console immediately. Isolation stops it reaching mapped-drive clients and other shares. Do not waste time blocking a callback domain: file encryption is local and does not need the internet to continue.

Keep the host powered on. Volatile memory can contain the encryption key, the ransomware's configuration, or artifacts that identify the strain and support recovery, and powering off destroys all of it. Isolate to stop the spread, preserve memory for forensics, and escalate as a declared ransomware incident.

subject: User granted a third-party app broad mailbox access via OAuth consent
ticketBody: Identity logs show a user clicked an OAuth 'Allow' prompt from an unfamiliar app that requested full mailbox read and send permissions. The app then started reading mail and creating inbox rules. No failed logins and no password reset happened, so a plain password change will not fix this.

evidence.label: Consent and activity, user rlopez
lines: OAuth consent granted to app 'MailAssistant Pro' (unverified publisher, registered 5 days ago)
Scopes granted: Mail.Read, Mail.Send, MailboxSettings.ReadWrite (full mailbox control)
Post-consent: app created an inbox rule auto-forwarding finance mail to an external address
No failed-login burst, no password change, MFA was satisfied normally by the user
The app's refresh token now lets it access the mailbox without the user or their password

commands.output: This is a consent-phishing (illicit grant) attack. The user was tricked into approving a malicious app. It now holds a refresh token with full mailbox scopes, so it keeps access even after a password reset or MFA. The fix is to revoke the app's grant and tokens, not just reset the password.
App created an auto-forward rule sending finance email to an external address. Remove the malicious rule as part of remediation.

goal: The account was compromised through an OAuth consent grant, not a stolen password. Contain it correctly.
hint: No password was ever stolen and MFA passed. A password reset alone leaves the app's token working. What actually cuts off this kind of access?

actions.label: Revoke the app's OAuth consent and refresh tokens, remove the malicious inbox rule, then review what the app accessed and reset the account as a precaution
actions.teach: Correct. This is consent phishing (an illicit grant). The attacker never needed the password: the user approved a malicious app that now holds a refresh token with full mailbox scopes. Revoking the app's consent and tokens is what actually severs access. Then delete the auto-forward rule it created and review the exposure. A password reset alone would leave the app's token fully working.

actions.label: Force a password reset on the account and close it
actions.teach: A password reset does not touch the app's OAuth refresh token, so the malicious app keeps reading and forwarding mail regardless. Consent-grant compromise is remediated by revoking the grant and tokens, not by rotating a password the attacker never had.

actions.label: MFA passed and there were no failed logins, so mark it a false positive
actions.teach: MFA passing is exactly why this is dangerous: the user themselves approved the grant, so no login looks abnormal. The attacker's access rides on the OAuth token, not a login, and there is an active auto-forward rule stealing finance mail. This is a real compromise.

kbArticle.title: OAuth consent-grant compromise: revoke the token, not just the password
body: Not every account takeover uses a stolen password. In a consent-phishing (illicit-grant) attack, a user is tricked into clicking 'Allow' on a malicious OAuth app that requests broad scopes like full mailbox read and send. The app receives a refresh token and can then read mail, send as the user, and create forwarding rules, all without ever knowing the password and without triggering failed-login or MFA alerts.

Because the access rides on the app's token rather than a login, a password reset does not remediate it. The token keeps working. The correct containment is to revoke the app's consent grant and its refresh/access tokens in the identity console, which is what actually severs its access.

After revoking, clean up what the app did: remove any malicious inbox or auto-forward rules it created, review which data it accessed, and reset the account as a precaution. Treat MFA-passing, login-clean identity alerts about unexpected app grants as real, not as false positives.

subject: Proprietary source code pushed to a public code repository
ticketBody: DLP flagged an outbound push: an internal repository containing proprietary source code, and what look like hardcoded credentials, was pushed to a personal public GitHub account from a corporate laptop. The repo is currently public and indexable. This carries legal, IP, and secret-exposure exposure.

evidence.label: DLP detail
lines: Source: developer laptop DEV-LAP-22, corporate network
Destination: github.com/<personal-account>/<repo>  (PUBLIC visibility)
Content: internal service source code + a config file with an AWS access key and a DB password
Classification: proprietary / secret; not an approved open-source release
Repo has been public for ~18 minutes; contains live-looking secrets

commands.output: Confirmed: proprietary source and hardcoded secrets are in a PUBLIC repo. Two clocks are running: the code exposure (needs legal/IR and takedown) and the leaked credentials (must be rotated, since anything public may already be scraped). This is an incident, not a coaching moment.
Exposed: one AWS access key and one database password. Public secrets should be treated as compromised the instant they are exposed. Rotate them regardless of takedown.

goal: Proprietary code and secrets are public right now. Do the containment that matters, in the right frame.
hint: Two problems: the code is exposed and the secrets are exposed. Deleting the repo fixes neither fully by itself. What has to happen no matter what?

actions.label: Escalate to IR (loop in legal and the code owner), initiate a takedown of the public repo, preserve evidence, and rotate the exposed AWS key and DB password immediately
actions.teach: Right call on both clocks. Proprietary code in a public repo is a data-exposure incident with legal and IP weight, so it goes through IR with legal and the code owner, plus a takedown request and evidence preservation. Separately, any secret exposed publicly is compromised the moment it is public and may already be scraped, so you rotate the AWS key and DB password now regardless of how fast the takedown lands.

actions.label: Message the developer and ask them to delete the public repo themselves
actions.teach: Deletion by the user does not un-expose anything already scraped, does not rotate the leaked secrets, and skips the legal and IR process a proprietary-code leak requires. It also relies on the person who caused the exposure to remediate it quietly. Escalate and rotate the secrets.

actions.label: Coach the developer on not committing secrets and close the ticket
actions.teach: Coaching is for later. Right now proprietary code and live-looking secrets are publicly indexable. Closing with only a talking-to leaves the IP exposed, the credentials valid, and legal unaware. This is a reportable incident: escalate, take it down, and rotate the secrets.

kbArticle.title: DLP: proprietary code or secrets exposed publicly
body: When DLP flags proprietary source code or secrets pushed to a public destination (a personal public repo, a paste site, personal cloud storage), treat it as a data-exposure incident, not a training issue. There are two independent problems: the intellectual-property exposure and any leaked credentials, and each has its own clock.

For the code exposure, escalate through incident response so legal and the code owner are engaged, request a takedown of the public content, and preserve evidence of what was exposed and by whom. Do not rely on the person who caused the leak to quietly delete it, and do not close with only coaching.

For leaked secrets, act independently and immediately: any credential exposed publicly must be considered compromised the moment it becomes public, because automated scrapers find public secrets within minutes. Rotate the exposed keys and passwords right away, regardless of how quickly the takedown succeeds. Deleting the repo does not un-leak a secret that was already scraped.

subject: VPN auth failures spiked 5x overnight, is it an attack?
ticketBody: A dashboard shows VPN authentication failures jumped to five times the normal rate overnight. It looks alarming. Read the logs carefully before you call it: the numbers alone do not tell you whether this is a password-spray attack or something mundane.

evidence.label: VPN auth failure breakdown, last 8h
lines: Failures up 5x, BUT: spread across ~1,900 distinct known-good employee usernames
Each username: 1 to 2 failures then a SUCCESS from the user's usual device and geo
Error code on the failures: 'auth timeout / gateway busy', not 'invalid credentials'
Timing lines up with a VPN gateway maintenance window announced by network ops
No single source IP dominates; no accounts locked out; no unusual geos

commands.output: Read the shape, not the headline. The failures are 'gateway busy' timeouts, spread thinly across ~1,900 legitimate users who then succeed from their normal devices, during an announced maintenance window. That is a performance blip, not credential attack. A password spray would show 'invalid credentials' against many accounts from a small set of sources, with few or no successes.
Network ops posted a VPN gateway maintenance window overnight. The failure spike lines up exactly with it. Correlating with the change calendar explains the spike.

goal: Interpret the log spike correctly instead of reacting to the top-line number.
hint: Look at the error type, the spread across users, and whether logins still succeed. Does this look like a spray, or like a busy gateway?

actions.label: Classify it as a benign performance blip from the announced maintenance window, document the reasoning, and set a threshold note so it doesn't re-alarm
actions.teach: Good log reading. The failures are 'gateway busy' timeouts spread thinly across ~1,900 legitimate users who then log in successfully from their usual devices, during an announced maintenance window. That is a performance artifact, not an attack. A real password spray shows 'invalid credentials' against many accounts from a few sources with few successes. Documenting the reasoning and tuning the threshold prevents the same false alarm tomorrow.

actions.label: Declare a password-spray attack and start locking accounts
actions.teach: The evidence contradicts a spray: the errors are gateway timeouts not credential failures, they are spread across known-good users who then succeed normally, and they coincide with a maintenance window. Locking accounts here would lock out real employees over a performance blip. Read the log shape before declaring.

actions.label: Mute the dashboard so the spike stops being annoying
actions.teach: Muting hides the signal without understanding it, so a genuine spike tomorrow would be silenced too. The right move is to explain this spike (a maintenance-window performance blip), document it, and tune the threshold, not blind the alert.

kbArticle.title: Reading an auth-log spike: shape over headline
body: A top-line number ('failures up 5x') is a prompt to investigate, not a verdict. The shape of the logs tells you whether a spike is an attack or an artifact. Read the error type, the spread across accounts, whether logins still succeed, the source diversity, and any correlating change.

A credential attack like password spraying looks specific: 'invalid credentials' errors, aimed at many accounts from a small set of source IPs, with few or no successes, often hitting accounts the users are not actively using. A benign blip looks different: timeout or 'gateway busy' errors spread thinly across many legitimate users who then succeed from their normal devices and locations, frequently lined up with a maintenance window or deploy on the change calendar.

When the shape says benign, document the reasoning and tune the alert threshold so the same performance artifact does not re-alarm. Do not lock accounts on a spike that is really a maintenance blip, and do not simply mute the dashboard, which would also silence a real spike later.

subject: Patient-zero found, but the attacker already moved to a second host
ticketBody: You confirmed the initial compromised host (WKS-31). But EDR shows the attacker used stolen credentials to authenticate from WKS-31 to a second machine, SRV-APP-09, ten minutes ago and is active there now. The team wants to isolate and clean WKS-31 and call it done. There is active lateral movement.

evidence.label: Lateral movement, current picture
lines: WKS-31 (patient zero): known compromised, attacker tooling present
10 min ago: successful remote logon WKS-31 -> SRV-APP-09 using dumped credentials
SRV-APP-09: attacker process active NOW, enumerating the domain
Same stolen service credential could reach other hosts that trust it
Only WKS-31 is currently isolated in the plan on the table

commands.output: The attacker is already on SRV-APP-09 and moving with a stolen credential that other hosts trust. Isolating only WKS-31 leaves the attacker with a live foothold and a valid credential to jump again. Containment has to cover every host they reached AND the credential path, not just patient zero.
The dumped credential is the pivot. Until it is disabled or reset, isolating hosts one by one just chases the attacker around the network.

goal: Contain an intrusion that has already spread. Do not stop at patient zero.
hint: Cleaning only WKS-31 leaves a live attacker on SRV-APP-09 with a credential that opens other doors. What has to be contained together?

actions.label: Isolate both WKS-31 and SRV-APP-09 together, disable/reset the stolen credential used to pivot, then scope for any further hosts before eradicating
actions.teach: Correct. Once lateral movement is confirmed, containment has to cover every host the attacker reached at the same time, not one at a time, or they simply pivot ahead of you. You also have to kill the pivot itself: disable or reset the stolen credential, because leaving it valid means isolating hosts one by one is a losing chase. Then scope for additional footholds before you eradicate anything.

actions.label: Isolate and reimage WKS-31, the original host, and close the incident
actions.teach: The attacker is no longer only on WKS-31. They are active on SRV-APP-09 right now with a stolen credential that reaches further. Cleaning patient zero while the intruder holds a live foothold and a valid credential just tells them you are onto them, while their access continues.

actions.label: Isolate SRV-APP-09 too, one host at a time as each alert comes in
actions.teach: Chasing hosts serially lets an active attacker stay a step ahead, and it ignores the real enabler: the stolen credential that any trusting host will accept. Contain the reached hosts together and disable the credential, then scope, rather than reacting one alert at a time.

kbArticle.title: Containing lateral movement: hosts and the credential, together
body: When an intrusion has already spread, containing patient zero is not containment. If the attacker used stolen credentials to move from the first host to a second and is active there, isolating only the original machine leaves them a live foothold and a valid way to jump again.

Contain every reached host at the same time rather than one at a time. Serial isolation lets an active, hands-on attacker stay a step ahead of you, pivoting to a new host before you finish with the last one.

Contain the pivot itself as well as the hosts. The enabler of lateral movement is usually a compromised credential that other systems trust, so disable or reset it. Otherwise you are chasing the attacker around a network whose doors still open for them. Once the reached hosts are isolated and the credential is killed, scope for any further footholds before you eradicate, so you do not miss persistence elsewhere.

subject: Confirmed-malicious file hash from one host, how far has it spread?
ticketBody: The file that triggered a detection on one workstation has been confirmed malicious by threat intel, with a known SHA-256 hash and a set of related indicators. The detected host is already contained. Now the question is scope: is this an isolated hit or is the same malware sitting quietly on other machines that never alerted?

evidence.label: IOC package from intel
lines: SHA-256 of the malicious binary (high-confidence, unique to this malware)
Related IOCs: a C2 domain, a mutex name, a registry autorun key it creates
Detected host WKS-88 is already isolated and preserved
EDR retains process-execution and file-write history fleet-wide
Open question: which other endpoints have executed or stored this exact file

commands.output: Pivot the confirmed SHA-256 across the whole fleet using EDR's historical telemetry, and corroborate with the related IOCs (C2 domain, mutex, autorun key). A hash search finds silent copies on hosts that never fired an alert. This turns one detection into a full scope of the outbreak.
Use the file hash as the primary anchor (it is unique and reliable), then confirm hits with the C2 domain, mutex, and autorun key. Multiple matching indicators raise confidence and reduce false hits.

goal: Use the confirmed indicators to find every host the malware touched, not just the one that alerted.
hint: One host alerted, but malware can sit silently on others. What do you do with a reliable file hash and a set of related IOCs?

actions.label: Sweep the entire fleet's historical EDR telemetry for the confirmed SHA-256, corroborate hits with the C2 domain, mutex, and autorun key, then contain every host that matches
actions.teach: That is threat hunting. One detection tells you the malware is present, not how widespread it is. Pivoting the confirmed file hash across the fleet's historical telemetry surfaces silent copies on hosts that never alerted, and corroborating with the related IOCs (C2 domain, mutex, autorun key) raises confidence. You scope the outbreak, then contain every host that matches, instead of assuming a single hit was the whole story.

actions.label: The one detected host is contained, so close the case
actions.teach: A single detection is a starting point, not the scope. Malware routinely sits on other machines without triggering an alert. Closing after one host, when you hold a reliable hash you could pivot across the fleet, risks leaving active infections in place. Hunt before you close.

actions.label: Add the hash to the blocklist and take no further action
actions.teach: Blocklisting the hash is worth doing, but it only prevents future execution of that exact file. It does not find the copies already resident and possibly running on other hosts. Blocking is prevention; you still need to hunt the existing indicators across the fleet to scope and contain what is already there.

kbArticle.title: IOC hunting: pivot a confirmed hash across the fleet
body: One detection tells you malware is present; it does not tell you how far it spread. Malware commonly sits on other endpoints without ever triggering an alert, so after a confirmed hit the job is to scope the outbreak, not to close on the single host that fired.

Pivot on the most reliable indicator you have. A high-confidence, unique file hash (SHA-256) is an excellent anchor: search the fleet's historical EDR telemetry for any host that executed or stored that exact file, including machines that never alerted. Corroborate matches with the related indicators from the intel package, such as the C2 domain, a mutex name, or an autorun registry key, since multiple agreeing indicators raise confidence and cut false hits.

Every host that matches gets contained, turning one detection into a complete picture of the intrusion. Blocklisting the hash is worth doing as prevention, but it only stops future execution of that file and does not find copies already resident, so it is a complement to hunting, not a substitute for it.

subject: canary for checkout v3.2.0 is red at 5% but the full rollout is queued to auto-promote in 4 min
ticketBody: The v3.2.0 deploy is going out as a canary. It has 5% of traffic and the pipeline is set to auto-promote to 100% in about four minutes if nobody intervenes. The canary's error rate and p95 are both clearly worse than the stable fleet, but only 5% of users are affected so far. The dashboard link is attached. What do you do before that timer fires?

evidence.label: Canary vs stable (last 10 min)
lines: stable  (95% traffic):  error 0.3%   p95 210ms
canary  (5% traffic):   error 6.1%   p95 940ms
canary errors are 5xx from the new /checkout/confirm path
auto-promote to 100% scheduled: T minus 3m50s

evidence.label: canary error sample
lines: 500 POST /checkout/confirm  "cannot read 'taxRate' of undefined"
only fires when the cart has a promo code applied
stable fleet (v3.1.4) does not hit this path

commands.output: canary v3.2.0 error 6.1% vs stable 0.3%, p95 940ms vs 210ms. The canary is measurably worse on the exact signals a canary exists to catch. Auto-promote fires in under 4 minutes.
canary 5xx trace: taxRate read on an undefined promo object in /checkout/confirm. Reproduces only with a promo code. It is a real regression in v3.2.0, not noise.

goal: Read the canary signal and make the right call before auto-promote fires.
hint: A canary exists so a bad release hurts 5% instead of 100%. The canary is clearly worse and a timer is about to promote it to everyone. Which lever is smallest and safest?

actions.label: Abort the promotion and roll the canary back to stable (v3.1.4)
actions.detail: Halt auto-promote, shift the 5% back to v3.1.4, and file the taxRate bug for a fixed re-canary.
actions.teach: That is the whole point of a canary. It caught a real regression at 5% blast radius, so you halt the promotion and roll that slice back to stable before the timer ships it to 100%. Then you fix the promo-code taxRate bug and canary again. Never promote a canary that is measurably worse than stable.

actions.label: Let it auto-promote, the sample is only 5% so the numbers are probably noise
actions.detail: Trust that a 6% error rate on 5% of traffic will average out once it is on 100%.
actions.teach: The signal is a 20x error rate and a 4x latency jump with a reproducible 5xx trace, not sampling noise. Promoting it means every user hits the taxRate bug on any promo checkout. A canary that looks worse than stable is telling you not to promote.

actions.label: Promote to 100% now and ship a hotfix right after
actions.detail: Get everyone onto v3.2.0, then push a forward fix for the taxRate crash.
actions.teach: You would deliberately ship a known-broken release to all users and then race an untested hotfix into a live fire. Rolling the canary back is instant and safe. Forward-fixing under fire is the exact anti-pattern rollback discipline exists to prevent.

kbArticle.title: Trust the canary: never promote a release that is worse than stable
body: A canary sends a small slice of traffic to the new release so a bad deploy hurts a few percent of users instead of everyone. Its only job is to compare the new version against the stable fleet on error rate and latency before you widen the rollout.

If the canary's error rate or p95 is clearly worse than stable, that is a signal to abort the promotion and roll the slice back, not to average the numbers away. Promoting anyway, or promoting-then-hotfixing, ships a known regression to 100% of users. Halt, roll back to the last good version, fix the bug, then canary again.

subject: errors spiking after the 09:40 deploy but the release also dropped a column, can we still roll back?
ticketBody: Release v4.0.0 went out at 09:40 and error rate jumped from 0.4% to 12%. Normally we would just roll back the code, but this release shipped a migration that DROPPED the legacy phone_number column, and the old v3.9.x code still reads that column. So rolling the deploy back to v3.9.x would make the OLD code crash on a column that no longer exists. The team is arguing about whether rollback is even possible. What is the safe move?

evidence.label: Timeline
lines: 09:40  deploy v4.0.0 (app code + migration 214)
09:41  migration 214 ran: ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN phone_number
09:43  error rate 0.4% -> 12%, all in the new profile serializer
old code (v3.9.x) still SELECTs users.phone_number

evidence.label: why plain rollback is unsafe
lines: rolling app back to v3.9.x => old code runs SELECT ... phone_number
column no longer exists => every profile read 500s
the destructive DROP is the reason a code-only rollback is a trap
the app 5xx is in v4.0.0's new serializer, separate from the drop

commands.output: v4.0.0 bundled a destructive migration: DROP COLUMN users.phone_number, in the same release as the app change. The old v3.9.x code still reads that column, so a code-only rollback would crash the old code on a missing column. This is why you never pair a destructive schema change with the code that needs it in one release.
The 12% spike is a bug in v4.0.0's new profile serializer, not the dropped column. So the app is broken forward and the schema is broken backward: you cannot simply roll the code to v3.9.x.

goal: Recover without crashing the old code on a column that was already dropped.
hint: The destructive DROP shipped WITH the code, so plain rollback would crash the old code on a missing column. What is the smallest change that makes the current code stop erroring without needing the dropped column back this second?

actions.label: Disable the new profile serializer via its flag (fall back to the last good serializer that does not read phone_number), keep the schema as-is
actions.detail: Flip the serializer flag off so the current v4.0.0 process stops 5xx-ing, staying on the new schema. Then plan the phone_number re-add as its own reversible migration and re-canary the serializer fix.
actions.teach: Correct. Because a destructive DROP shipped in the same release, a code-only rollback would crash the OLD code on the missing column, so plain rollback is a trap. The 5xx is a bug in the new serializer, which is behind a flag, so flipping that flag off stops the bleeding without touching the schema and without needing the column back. Then you fix forward calmly. The real lesson: never bundle a destructive migration with the code that depends on it. Use expand-and-contract so rollback stays safe.

actions.label: Roll the app back to v3.9.x to be safe
actions.detail: Standard incident reflex: revert to the last known-good release.
actions.teach: Rollback is usually right, but here the release already DROPPED phone_number and v3.9.x still reads it, so reverting the code makes the old code 500 on a column that no longer exists. You would turn a 12% error rate into a full profile outage. When a destructive migration shipped with the code, code-only rollback is not safe.

actions.label: Immediately re-run a migration to add phone_number back, then roll the code back
actions.detail: Restore the column so the old code has something to read, then revert to v3.9.x.
actions.teach: Re-adding a just-dropped column under an active incident risks a lock on a hot table and gives you back an empty column with no data, so the old code reads nulls anyway. It is slower and riskier than flipping off the one flagged serializer that is actually throwing. Stop the bleeding at the code, then plan the schema change properly.

kbArticle.title: Expand and contract: keep rollback safe across schema changes
body: A destructive migration (dropping a column, renaming, tightening a constraint) shipped in the SAME release as the code that needs it makes rollback a trap: revert the code and the old version crashes on a schema that already changed. Recovery then has to go forward, not back.

The safe pattern is expand-and-contract. First expand: add the new column or table and write to both old and new in a release that still works if rolled back. Only after the new code is stable and the old column is unused do you contract in a later release: drop the old column. Because expand and contract are separate deploys, every version in between is rollback-safe. When an incident hits a release that violated this, stop the bleeding with the smallest lever the current code offers (a flag, a config flip) rather than a code-only rollback that fights the new schema.

subject: our sync worker is getting 429s from the payments API and now some charges are missing
ticketBody: Our nightly reconciliation worker calls the payments provider's API in a tight loop. Since we grew, it now blasts about 40 requests per second and the provider started returning 429 Too Many Requests. Our code treats a 429 as a hard failure and skips that record, so some charges are silently missing from the reconciliation. The provider's docs say the limit is 25 req/s and every 429 includes a Retry-After header. How should the worker handle this?

evidence.label: worker log sample
lines: POST /v1/charges/reconcile  -> 200 (x1900)
POST /v1/charges/reconcile  -> 429 Too Many Requests
Retry-After: 2
X-RateLimit-Limit: 25   X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0
our handler: catch(429) => log 'skip', continue   // record dropped

evidence.label: the numbers
lines: we send ~40 req/s, documented limit is 25 req/s
every 429 carries Retry-After telling us exactly how long to wait
dropped-on-429 records are the missing charges
no auth or account problem, purely volume over the ceiling

commands.output: The 429 responses carry Retry-After: 2 and X-RateLimit-Limit: 25 with Remaining: 0. We are sending ~40 req/s against a documented 25 req/s ceiling. A 429 is a back-off signal, not a permanent failure, and it tells us exactly how long to wait.
Current handler catches 429 and skips the record, which is why charges go missing. There is no throttle on the outbound rate and no retry that honors Retry-After.

goal: Stop dropping records and stay under the provider's rate limit.
hint: A 429 is not a failure, it is 'slow down', and the response literally tells you how long via Retry-After. What should the worker do instead of skipping the record?

actions.label: Throttle outbound to under 25 req/s and retry 429s with backoff, honoring Retry-After
actions.detail: Add a client-side rate limiter capped below the documented 25 req/s, and on a 429 wait the Retry-After duration then retry the same record instead of skipping it. Backfill the previously dropped charges.
actions.teach: Right. A 429 means you exceeded the rate limit, and the Retry-After header tells you exactly how long to pause. Throttle your own outbound rate under the documented ceiling so you rarely hit 429, and when you do, back off and retry the SAME record rather than dropping it. Then backfill the charges that were skipped. That keeps reconciliation complete and keeps you a good API citizen.

actions.label: Remove the delay and just retry 429s immediately in a tight loop
actions.detail: On a 429, retry right away so no record is skipped.
actions.teach: Retrying instantly ignores Retry-After and keeps you over the limit, so the provider returns more 429s and may escalate to a longer ban. Hammering harder is the opposite of what a 429 asks for. Slow down under the ceiling and honor the back-off the response gives you.

actions.label: Email the provider to raise our limit and leave the code as-is
actions.detail: Ask for a higher quota so 40 req/s stops getting throttled.
actions.teach: A higher quota might help later, but it does nothing today and your code still drops records the moment it hits any ceiling. Fix the client to throttle and retry-with-backoff first so reconciliation is correct regardless of the limit. A limit increase is a follow-up, not the fix.

kbArticle.title: Handle HTTP 429 with client-side throttling and Retry-After backoff
body: HTTP 429 Too Many Requests means you exceeded a rate limit. It is a transient back-off signal, not a permanent failure, and a well-behaved API returns a Retry-After header (and often X-RateLimit-* headers) telling you exactly how long to wait. Treating a 429 as a hard error and dropping the record silently loses work.

Two fixes, together: throttle your own outbound rate to stay under the documented ceiling (a client-side rate limiter or token bucket), and when you do get a 429, wait the Retry-After duration and retry the SAME request rather than skipping it. Add exponential backoff with jitter for repeated 429s. Then backfill anything already dropped. Asking the provider for a higher quota is a slower follow-up, not a substitute for handling the limit correctly.

subject: users say their profile edits do not save, but they actually do, then revert
ticketBody: A bunch of users report that when they change their display name, it saves, but a minute later it shows the old name again. Support confirmed the database DOES have the new name. The old name keeps coming back on reads. We added a Redis cache to the profile read endpoint two weeks ago to cut database load. Nobody has deployed since. Can you figure out why the stale name keeps winning?

evidence.label: what we observe
lines: PUT /profile writes new name to Postgres: confirmed correct in DB
GET /profile returns the OLD name for up to ~10 minutes after the edit
profile reads were cached in Redis 2 weeks ago (TTL 600s)
the write path updates Postgres but never touches the cache

evidence.label: cache config
lines: cache key: profile:{userId}   TTL: 600s
GET /profile: read-through (cache miss -> DB -> set cache)
PUT /profile: writes DB only, no cache delete or update
stale entry serves until the 600s TTL expires => old name returns

commands.output: Redis holds profile:{userId} with the OLD name and a 600s TTL. The write path updated Postgres but never invalidated or updated this key, so reads keep returning the cached old value until the TTL lapses. Classic stale-cache-on-write.
PUT /profile does UPDATE users SET display_name = ... and returns. There is no cache.del(profile:{userId}) or cache set after the write. The read cache and the write path are not coordinated.

goal: Make reads reflect writes without ripping out the cache.
hint: The database is right, the cache is wrong, and nothing updates the cache when the record changes. What is missing on the write path?

actions.label: Invalidate (or update) the cache key on write: delete profile:{userId} inside the PUT after the DB commit
actions.detail: On a successful profile update, delete or overwrite the profile:{userId} cache entry so the next read repopulates from the fresh database row.
actions.teach: That is the fix. A read-through cache is only correct if writes keep it in sync. The write updated the database but left the stale key in Redis, so reads served the old name until the TTL expired. Deleting (or updating) the cache key on write means the next read repopulates from the fresh row. Keep the cache, just invalidate it when the underlying data changes.

actions.label: Set the cache TTL to a few seconds so stale data clears fast
actions.detail: Shorten the TTL from 600s to ~5s so the old name only lingers briefly.
actions.teach: A tiny TTL just shrinks the stale window, it does not fix correctness: users can still see the old name for those seconds, and you throw away almost all of the cache's benefit, hammering the database you added the cache to protect. Invalidate on write and keep a sensible TTL.

actions.label: Remove the Redis cache entirely
actions.detail: Rip out the cache so every read goes straight to Postgres and is always fresh.
actions.teach: That trades a correctness bug for the load problem the cache was added to solve, and it is an overreaction to a one-line miss. The cache is fine, the write path just needs to invalidate it. Do not delete a working optimization to avoid coordinating it.

kbArticle.title: Keep a cache correct: invalidate on write
body: A read-through cache serves a stored copy of the data. If a write updates the source of truth but does not update or delete the cached copy, reads keep returning the old value until the TTL expires. The database is correct while the cache lies, which shows up as edits that seem to save then revert.

The fix is cache invalidation on write: after a successful write to the database, delete (or overwrite) the affected cache key so the next read repopulates from the fresh row. Shortening the TTL only shrinks the stale window and wastes the cache's benefit, and removing the cache trades correctness for the load problem it solved. Keep the cache and coordinate it with writes.

subject: search API returning 503s under load, thread pool exhausted waiting on a slow dependency
ticketBody: The search API started throwing 503s during the lunch peak. It calls a downstream recommendations service that has gotten slow (p99 now 8 seconds). Our search service has NO timeout on that call, so requests pile up waiting, the request thread pool fills, and then healthy requests that do not even need recommendations start failing too. The recommendations part is optional, it just adds 'you might also like'. How do we stop search from falling over when a non-critical dependency is slow?

evidence.label: what is happening
lines: recommendations service p99: 8s (was 120ms last week)
search calls it with NO client timeout => threads block up to 8s
request thread pool (200) fully occupied waiting on recs
healthy /search requests now 503 because no threads are free

evidence.label: dependency criticality
lines: recommendations is OPTIONAL: it only adds a 'you might also like' row
core search results do not need it at all
one slow optional call is taking down the whole endpoint
no timeout, no fallback, no isolation on the recs call

commands.output: Thread dump: ~190 of 200 request threads are BLOCKED inside the recommendations HTTP client, waiting on a socket read. No timeout is set, so each blocks for the full 8s p99. The pool is starved, so unrelated requests get 503s. The slow OPTIONAL dependency is the root cause.

recommendations p99 climbed to 8s. It is not on the critical path: search works fine without the 'you might also like' row. The problem is that our code lets an optional slow call block core requests.

goal: Keep search up when an optional dependency is slow.
hint: An OPTIONAL call with no timeout is starving the thread pool. What two things should every outbound call to a non-critical dependency have?

actions.label: Add a tight timeout to the recommendations call and fall back to results-without-recs on timeout (fail open)
actions.detail: Set a short client timeout (well under the request budget) on the recs call, and on timeout or error return the core search results without the 'you might also like' row. Add a circuit breaker so repeated failures skip the call entirely.
actions.teach: Correct. An optional dependency must never be able to take down the core endpoint. A tight timeout stops threads from blocking for 8s, and a fallback (return search results without recommendations) means a slow recs service degrades one feature instead of failing the whole request. A circuit breaker then stops even attempting the call while it is unhealthy. Timeout plus fallback plus isolation is how you keep the 5xx contained to the optional path.

actions.label: Scale the search service to more instances to add thread capacity
actions.detail: Add pods so there are more request threads to absorb the blocked calls.
actions.teach: More instances just give you more threads to block on the same 8s call, so they fill up too, at higher cost. The dependency is slow and optional; the fix is to stop letting it block core requests, not to buy more threads to waste waiting. Add a timeout and a fallback.

actions.label: Restart the recommendations service and wait for it to recover
actions.detail: Bounce recs so its latency drops back to normal, which unblocks search.
actions.teach: Even if a restart helps recs today, search is still one slow optional dependency away from another outage because it has no timeout and no fallback. You would be fixing the symptom on someone else's service instead of making your own endpoint resilient. Isolate the optional call so search survives a slow recs regardless.

kbArticle.title: Timeouts and fallbacks: stop a slow dependency from becoming a 5xx
body: A slow downstream call with no timeout blocks a request thread for as long as the dependency takes. Under load the thread pool fills with blocked requests and the whole endpoint starts returning 503s, even for requests that never needed that dependency. One slow call becomes a full outage.

Every outbound call needs a timeout that fits within the request's overall budget, so a thread is never held hostage. For a non-critical dependency, pair the timeout with a fallback that fails open: return the core response without the optional part. Add a circuit breaker so repeated failures skip the unhealthy call entirely. Scaling up or restarting the dependency treats the symptom; timeout plus fallback plus isolation makes your endpoint survive a slow dependency by design.

subject: writes to orders timing out, a migration is holding an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on the hot table
ticketBody: Someone kicked off a migration that runs ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending' on Postgres. orders is our hottest table. Every INSERT and UPDATE to orders is now timing out and checkout is failing. The migration is still running because the DEFAULT forces a full table rewrite while holding an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock, and it is queued behind live traffic. What do you do right now, and how should this have been done?

evidence.label: pg_locks / activity
lines: pid 4412: ALTER TABLE orders ...  state: active   duration: 3m12s
lock: AccessExclusiveLock on orders  (granted)
~180 sessions: state 'active', wait_event Lock, on INSERT/UPDATE orders
checkout write p99: timing out (was 40ms)

evidence.label: why it locked
lines: ADD COLUMN ... NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending' on old Postgres rewrites every row
the rewrite holds ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, blocking all reads and writes
the safe form is: add nullable column, backfill in batches, then set NOT NULL
the lock, not disk or CPU, is what is timing out checkout

commands.output: pid 4412 holds AccessExclusiveLock on orders and has for 3+ minutes. ~180 INSERT/UPDATE sessions are stuck with wait_event=Lock behind it. The ALTER with a NOT NULL DEFAULT is rewriting the whole hot table under an exclusive lock. That lock is what is timing out checkout, not resource pressure.

The migration is ADD COLUMN status NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending', which on this table forces a full rewrite. The safe rewrite is: add the column nullable with no default, backfill in small batches, then add the NOT NULL constraint. Cancelling the running statement releases the lock immediately.

goal: Release the lock now, then land the column the safe way.
hint: One statement is holding an exclusive lock on the hot table and every write is queued behind it. What stops the bleeding fastest, and what is the non-locking way to add the column?

actions.label: Cancel the running migration to release the lock, then re-do it safely (nullable column, batched backfill, then NOT NULL)
actions.detail: Terminate the blocking ALTER so the exclusive lock drops and queued writes drain. Then add the column nullable with no default, backfill 'pending' in small batches, and finally add the NOT NULL constraint, off-peak.
actions.teach: Correct. The exclusive lock from the table-rewriting ALTER is what is timing out every write, so cancelling that one statement releases the lock immediately and checkout recovers. Then you land the column without a rewrite: add it nullable, backfill the default in batches so no single statement holds a long lock, and only then set NOT NULL. A destructive or rewriting DDL on a hot table must be split into non-locking steps and run off-peak.

actions.label: Let the migration finish, it will release the lock when it is done
actions.detail: Do not interrupt it; the rewrite will complete and writes will resume.
actions.teach: A full rewrite of the hottest table can take many minutes, and checkout is failing for every second of it. Waiting means a prolonged write outage and possibly a growing lock queue that itself causes cascading timeouts. Cancel the statement to release the lock now, then land the change safely off the critical path.

actions.label: Restart the database to clear the stuck locks
actions.detail: Bounce Postgres so the lock and the queued sessions reset.
actions.teach: Restarting the primary is a far bigger hammer: it drops every connection, may trigger crash recovery and a failover, and can leave the migration half-applied. Cancelling the single blocking statement releases the lock cleanly with no outage of the whole database. Never reboot the box when you can cancel the one offending query.

kbArticle.title: Run schema migrations on hot tables without long locks
body: Some DDL takes an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock and rewrites the whole table. Adding a NOT NULL column with a DEFAULT on older Postgres is the classic example: it rewrites every row while holding a lock that blocks all reads and writes. On a hot table this queues every live query behind it, and the symptom is write timeouts even though CPU and disk look fine, because the bottleneck is the lock, not resources.

When one is already stuck, cancel that single statement to release the lock immediately rather than waiting it out or restarting the database. To land the change safely, split it into non-locking steps: add the column nullable with no default, backfill the value in small batches so no statement holds a long lock, then add the NOT NULL constraint, and build indexes CONCURRENTLY. Run heavy DDL off-peak. Never pair a table-rewriting migration with live peak traffic.

subject: error spike started exactly when someone flipped the new_pricing_engine flag to 100%
ticketBody: Error rate jumped from 0.5% to 22% at 16:10. No deploy went out. The audit log shows that at 16:10 someone flipped the new_pricing_engine feature flag from a 5% ramp straight to 100% for everyone. The new pricing engine throws on any order with a bundled discount, which the 5% cohort apparently never happened to include. Nothing was built or shipped, this was a config change. What is the fastest safe fix?

evidence.label: flag audit log
lines: 16:09  new_pricing_engine: rollout 5%  (stable for 3 days)
16:10  new_pricing_engine: rollout 5% -> 100%   by: batch-config-push
16:10  error rate 0.5% -> 22%, all in PricingEngine.applyBundle()
no code deploy in the window: this was a pure config flip

evidence.label: the failure
lines: new engine throws on orders with a bundled discount
the 5% cohort had not hit a bundled order, so the ramp looked clean
flipping to 100% exposed every bundled-discount order at once
the flag is the single change that caused the incident

commands.output: The audit log shows new_pricing_engine went 5% -> 100% at 16:10, and the error spike starts the same minute. No deploy happened. The flag flip is the exact change that caused the incident, and it is configuration, so it reverts in seconds.

Every 5xx is PricingEngine.applyBundle() throwing on orders with a bundled discount. The old pricing path (flag off) does not hit this code. Turning the flag back to off restores the working path immediately.

goal: Stop the bleeding with the smallest, fastest lever, then fix the engine.
hint: No code shipped. One config value changed and the errors started that same minute. What is faster and safer than any deploy?

actions.label: Flip new_pricing_engine back off (or to the last-good 5%) to restore the old pricing path
actions.detail: Revert the exact flag that caused the spike. It is config, so it takes effect in seconds with nothing to build. Then fix applyBundle() and re-ramp carefully, including a bundled-discount test cohort.
actions.teach: Exactly right. A feature flag is the smallest safe lever there is: the incident was caused by a config flip, so flipping it back off reverts the exact change in seconds with nothing to deploy. That instantly restores the working old pricing path. Then you fix applyBundle() and re-ramp slowly, this time making sure the ramp cohort actually includes bundled-discount orders. Revert the precise change that caused the incident, do not reach for a bigger tool.

actions.label: Roll back the last deploy to the previous release
actions.detail: Revert to the prior build to undo the change.
actions.teach: Nothing was deployed, so there is no code change to roll back, and a redeploy is slower than a flag flip and might come back up with the flag still at 100%, keeping the errors. The incident was a config flip; revert the config. Match your lever to the change that actually caused the fire.

actions.label: Write and deploy a hotfix to PricingEngine.applyBundle()
actions.detail: Fix the bundled-discount crash and ship it forward under the incident.
actions.teach: Forward-fixing under an active incident ships an untested change into a fire and takes a full build-and-deploy while users keep hitting 22% errors. You already have an instant off switch: the flag. Flip it off to recover now, then fix applyBundle() calmly off the critical path.

kbArticle.title: A feature flag is the smallest safe lever, and its blast radius is the ramp
body: When a single feature flag flip causes an error spike, the fastest safe fix is to flip it back off. It is configuration, not code, so the change reverts in seconds with nothing to build or deploy, and it restores the exact working path that existed before the flip. A full redeploy is slower and may come back up with the flag still on.

The deeper lesson is blast radius. A clean 5% ramp only proves the code works for the traffic that ramp happened to see. Jumping straight to 100% exposes every untested case at once, like the bundled-discount order the small cohort never hit. Ramp gradually, make sure each stage's cohort covers the risky input shapes, and always keep the flag as your instant off switch for the exact change you made.

subject: email queue backlog at 400k and climbing, one poison message is crash-looping the worker
ticketBody: The outbound email queue has backed up to 400,000 messages and is growing by thousands a minute. Password-reset and receipt emails are delayed by over an hour. The consumer throughput has collapsed to almost zero. Digging in: one specific message with a malformed recipient makes the worker throw, and because the worker crashes before ack-ing, that same message is redelivered to the head of the queue and crashes the next worker too. It is a poison message stuck at the front, blocking everything behind it. There is no dead-letter queue configured. How do you drain the backlog?

evidence.label: queue + worker state
lines: queue depth: 400,231 and climbing ~3k/min
consumer throughput: ~0 msg/s (was 1,200/s)
worker log: 'invalid recipient' -> crash -> restart -> same message redelivered
no DLQ / no max-retry: the poison message loops at the head forever

evidence.label: the poison message
lines: msg id 88f1: recipient '' (empty), throws in the address validator
worker dies before ack => broker re-queues it to the front
every worker picks it up next and dies the same way
the other 400k messages are fine, they are just stuck behind it

commands.output: Queue depth 400k+ and climbing, consumer throughput near zero. The worker keeps crashing on the same message and cannot make progress, so nothing behind it drains. This is a poison-message backlog, not a capacity problem.

msg 88f1 has an empty recipient and throws in the validator. The worker crashes before ack, so the broker redelivers 88f1 to the head and the next worker dies on it too. There is no dead-letter queue and no retry cap, so it loops forever, blocking the 400k good messages behind it.

goal: Get the queue draining again without losing the good messages.
hint: One bad message is crash-looping at the head and it has no retry cap and no dead-letter queue. What lets the worker move past it so the other 400k can flow?

actions.label: Route the poison message to a dead-letter queue (catch the parse failure, ack it off to a DLQ after N retries) so the worker skips it and drains the rest
actions.detail: Make the worker catch the validation error, route msg 88f1 to a dead-letter queue after a retry cap instead of crashing, so it acks and moves on. Configure a DLQ and max-retry going forward, then scale consumers to burn down the 400k backlog and inspect 88f1 separately.
actions.teach: Correct. A poison message that crashes the worker before ack gets redelivered forever and blocks everything behind it. The fix is to let the worker survive a bad message: catch the failure, and after a retry cap route that one message to a dead-letter queue and ack past it. The moment the worker stops dying on 88f1, the 400k good messages drain. Then you add a DLQ and max-retry so no single bad message can ever stall the pipeline again, and you inspect the poison message offline.

actions.label: Scale the workers from 4 to 40 to chew through the backlog faster
actions.detail: Add consumers so the queue drains 10x faster.
actions.teach: Every one of those 40 workers will pick up the same poison message at the head and crash on it, so you get 40 crash loops and still near-zero throughput at ten times the cost. The backlog is not a capacity problem, it is one message that no worker can get past. Skip the poison message first, then scale to drain the rest.

actions.label: Purge the whole queue to clear the backlog and start fresh
actions.detail: Drop all 400k messages so the pipeline is empty and workers recover.
actions.teach: Purging destroys 400k legitimate emails: password resets, receipts, real user actions, to get rid of one bad message. That is data loss to avoid handling a single poison record. Dead-letter the one message that is blocking the head and the rest will drain intact. Never nuke the queue to dodge a poison message.

kbArticle.title: Drain a queue backlog caused by a poison message
body: A poison message is one the consumer cannot process without throwing. If the worker crashes before it acknowledges the message, the broker redelivers it to the head of the queue, the next worker dies on it too, and throughput collapses to zero. The backlog then grows even though the other messages are perfectly fine, they are just stuck behind the one the worker keeps choking on.

The fix is to stop the worker from dying on it: catch the processing error and, after a bounded number of retries, route that message to a dead-letter queue and acknowledge past it so the consumer moves on. The instant the worker skips the poison message, the rest of the backlog drains. Adding more workers only multiplies the crash loop, and purging the queue throws away every good message to avoid handling one bad one. Configure a dead-letter queue and a max-retry limit so a single bad message can never stall the pipeline, then scale consumers to burn down what remains.

subject: You are booked to test 'the customer portal' starting Monday. Are you cleared to begin?

email.body: A client wants you to test 'the customer portal' next week. On a call the ops lead said 'yeah go for it, hit whatever you find.' You have an email thread but no signed document. The portal talks to a payment gateway, a login service on a shared domain, and a marketing site, and you are not sure which of those you are allowed to touch. Monday is close.

evidence.label: What you actually have
lines: A verbal 'go for it' from the ops lead on a call
An email thread, but no signed rules-of-engagement document
No explicit IP ranges, no in/out-of-scope host list, no testing window
The portal depends on a third-party payment gateway and a shared login domain
No named emergency contact if something breaks during the test

goal: Get authorization pinned down before any testing begins.
hint: A friendly 'go for it' on a call is not a signed scope. What has to exist in writing before Monday?

actions.label: Hold off on testing and get a signed rules-of-engagement document that lists the exact in-scope hosts, the excluded third parties, the window, allowed techniques, and an emergency contact
actions.teach: Correct. A verbal go is not authorization. You pause, get the scope, exclusions, window, and an emergency contact signed by someone empowered to grant it, and you explicitly clarify that the third-party gateway and shared login domain are off limits unless named in scope. That signed document is the entire legal basis for the test.

actions.label: Start testing Monday on the strength of the verbal go, since the client clearly wants it done
actions.teach: A verbal 'go for it' does not authorize you to touch anything, and testing without a signed scope is unauthorized access no matter how friendly the client sounds. If it breaks, or if you drift onto the payment gateway, there is nothing protecting you. Get it in writing first.

actions.label: Assume 'the portal' includes the payment gateway and shared login domain and test all of them to be thorough
actions.teach: Assuming scope is how testers end up hacking systems the client never controlled and never authorized. The payment gateway is almost certainly a separate company. Never expand scope by assumption. Clarify in writing, then test only what is named.

kbArticle.title: Rules of engagement: pin the scope before you touch anything
body: Before a single packet leaves your machine, the rules of engagement must be signed: which hosts, IP ranges, and domains are in scope, which are explicitly excluded, the testing window, allowed techniques, and who to call in an emergency. A verbal go from a friendly manager is not authorization. Get it in writing from someone empowered to grant it.

Ambiguity is the enemy. If the target list says 'the app' but not which subdomains or third-party dependencies count, you clarify in writing first. An engagement that starts with a fuzzy scope ends with a legal problem. The signed document is what stands between authorized testing and a computer-misuse charge.

subject: Subdomain enumeration on the in-scope domain returns hosts you were never told about
ticketBody: Scope names one host: app.client.com. Passive subdomain enumeration on client.com returns a pile of extra names, including a staging server, an old acquired brand on a different domain, and what looks like an internal admin box. None of them are in the signed scope. Some look soft.

evidence.label: Enumeration output vs signed scope
lines: IN SCOPE (signed): app.client.com ONLY
Found via passive DNS: staging.client.com, admin-internal.client.com
Found: legacy.oldbrand.io (an acquired company, different domain)
admin-internal.client.com has no login on the banner and looks soft
None of these appear in the rules-of-engagement document
