commands.output: Passive DNS for client.com returns app, staging, admin-internal, plus legacy.oldbrand.io. Only app.client.com is named in the signed scope. Discovery is passive; probing these actively would be out of scope.

goal: Decide what to do with the newly discovered but out-of-scope hosts.
hint: Finding a host is not authorization to test it. Which single host may you actively probe right now?

actions.label: Actively test only app.client.com, record the other discovered hosts as observations, and ask the client to confirm or extend scope in writing before touching any of them
actions.teach: That is the disciplined recon boundary. Passive discovery is fine, but the signed scope, not DNS, decides what you may actively test. You note the staging box, the internal admin host, and the acquired-brand domain as observations and let the client authorize them in writing. The soft-looking admin box is exactly the trap that ends careers when someone assumes discovery equals permission.

actions.label: The internal admin box looks unauthenticated, so probe it now while it is easy
actions.teach: Easy and authorized are different things. admin-internal.client.com is not in the signed scope, so probing it is unauthorized access even though it belongs to the same company. Record it, report it, and wait for written authorization. Never let a soft target pull you across the scope line.

actions.label: Since they all trace back to the client, treat every discovered host as fair game and test them all
actions.teach: The acquired-brand domain is a separate company on a different domain and may not be the client's to authorize at all. Tracing back to a name is not the same as being in scope. Test only what the document names, and get the rest authorized in writing first.

kbArticle.title: Recon turns up more than you are allowed to test
body: Passive recon on an in-scope domain routinely surfaces related assets: extra subdomains, a staging box, an acquired company on a different domain, a cloud bucket. Discovering an asset is not the same as being authorized to test it. The scope document, not DNS, decides what you may probe.

When enumeration reveals hosts outside the signed scope, the disciplined move is to record them as observations and confirm authorization before touching them. Many of these belong to third parties or other business units who never agreed to your test. Enumerate freely from public data, but active testing stops at the scope boundary.

subject: You have four confirmed findings. Which one goes to the top of the report?
ticketBody: Testing is done and you confirmed four issues. You need to rank them by severity so the client fixes the worst one first. They are all real; the question is which does the most damage in the wrong hands.

evidence.label: Confirmed findings
lines: A) Unauthenticated remote code execution on the internet-facing app server
B) Reflected XSS that only fires against the victim's own session (self-XSS)
C) Missing HSTS response header on the marketing site
D) A verbose stack trace on a 500 error page (info disclosure, low detail)
Impact is what ranks these, not novelty

commands.output: Impact and exploitability: (A) pre-auth RCE, internet-facing, attacker gains the whole host with no credentials. (B) self-XSS needs the victim to attack themselves. (C) missing header, no direct compromise. (D) minor info leak. (A) dominates on every axis.

goal: Rank the findings so the highest-impact issue is remediated first.
hint: Ask who can trigger each one, from where, with what access, and what they gain. Which one hands an unauthenticated attacker the whole box?

actions.label: Rank the unauthenticated RCE (A) as Critical and top of the report, then order the rest by real impact, flagging the self-XSS and missing header as Low
actions.teach: That is severity triage done by impact. A pre-authentication RCE on an internet-facing host gives any attacker the whole system with no credentials, so it dominates every other finding and leads the report. Self-XSS that only harms the victim and a missing header are genuinely Low. Ranking by consistent impact is what makes the report worth acting on.

actions.label: Put the XSS at the top because cross-site scripting always sounds serious to clients
actions.teach: Severity is impact, not vibes. This XSS only fires against the victim's own session, so a real attacker cannot use it to harm anyone else. Meanwhile a pre-auth RCE is sitting open. Rank by who can exploit it and what they gain, and the RCE clearly leads.

actions.label: Mark all four as High so the client takes every one of them seriously
actions.teach: Flattening everything to High destroys the signal the client needs to spend a limited remediation budget well. If a missing header and an unauthenticated RCE look equally urgent, the RCE may wait behind trivia. Triage honestly so the worst issue is unmistakably first.

kbArticle.title: Severity triage: rank findings by real impact, not by how clever they are
body: When a test produces several findings, severity is driven by impact and exploitability, not by how interesting the bug is. A pre-authentication remote code execution on an internet-facing host outranks a self-only XSS or a missing security header every time, because the first hands an unauthenticated attacker the whole system.

Use a consistent rubric (for example CVSS) and reason about who can trigger it, from where, and what they gain. Rank the report so the client fixes the things that would hurt most first. A tidy but mis-prioritized report wastes the client's remediation budget on low-impact noise while a critical sits open.

subject: On day one you find a critical that exposes customer data. Do you wait for the report?

phone.opener: It is day one of a five-day engagement. You just confirmed an unauthenticated endpoint that returns other customers' records on the internet-facing, in-scope app. The rules of engagement list an emergency security contact. Four more days of testing remain. What do you do with this right now?

followups.label: Ask: what does the rules-of-engagement document say to do with a critical mid-test?
followups.reply: It names a security contact and an emergency channel and asks you to notify them immediately for anything critical or actively dangerous.

followups.label: Ask: is anyone actively exploiting it right now?
followups.reply: You have no evidence of live exploitation, but the endpoint is open to the whole internet and trivially reachable.

followups.label: Ask: can you just fix it yourself since you are already in there?
followups.reply: You are authorized to test, not to change their production systems. Touching their config is out of scope.

evidence.label: The situation
lines: Unauthenticated endpoint leaks other customers' records (in scope, internet-facing)
Day one of five; the full report is not due for days
Rules of engagement name a security contact and an emergency channel
No evidence of live exploitation yet, but it is trivially reachable
You are authorized to test, not to modify their systems

goal: Handle a critical finding that is too dangerous to sit in a draft report for days.
hint: Some findings cannot wait until Friday's report. There is an emergency contact in the rules of engagement for a reason.

actions.label: Notify the client's designated emergency contact immediately with the finding and the risk, recommend they take the endpoint offline, then continue in-scope testing without exploiting it further
actions.teach: Right call. A critical exposing customer data is exactly what the emergency contact in the rules of engagement is for. You notify them now so they can pull the endpoint before an attacker finds it, you do not keep pulling records, and you do not patch their production yourself. Early escalation is a notification, not a license to go deeper.

actions.label: Log it and save it for the written report at the end of the week like everything else
actions.teach: Sitting on an internet-facing critical that is leaking customer data for four days is negligent. If it is exploited before Friday, that exposure is on you for staying silent. Some findings are too dangerous to wait. Use the emergency contact the rules of engagement gave you.

actions.label: Quietly fix the endpoint yourself since you are already inside their system
actions.teach: You are authorized to test, not to modify their production systems. Changing their config, even to help, is out of scope and can break things you do not understand and destroy evidence. Report it to the emergency contact and let their team remediate.

kbArticle.title: When a finding cannot wait for the report
body: Most findings belong in the written report at the end of the engagement. A few are so severe, and so likely to be exploited before the report ships, that sitting on them is negligent. An unauthenticated critical on an internet-facing system, or evidence that customer data is already exposed, warrants an immediate heads-up to the client's designated contact through the agreed emergency channel.

Escalating early is a notification, not a license to go further. You tell the client what you found and what to do to reduce risk now, you do not keep exploiting, and you do not attempt to patch their production system yourself. The rules of engagement name the emergency contact for exactly this moment. Use it, then continue testing in scope.

subject: Your proof-of-concept just dumped a screen of real customers' health records
ticketBody: You were confirming an access-control flaw on the in-scope app. The request you sent to prove it returned a full page of real customers' names, addresses, and health information, more than you expected. You have plenty to demonstrate the bug. What do you do with the data now?

evidence.label: What just happened
lines: A proof-of-concept request returned real customer PII including health data
You already have enough to prove the access-control flaw exists
Continuing to pull records adds no proof, only exposure and liability
Real sensitive data must not be copied off the client's systems
The rules of engagement specify a channel for sensitive-data incidents

goal: Handle real sensitive data you accessed without turning a finding into a breach.
hint: You already proved the bug. More records prove nothing extra. What do you do with what you touched?

actions.label: Stop pulling data, do not copy the records, document the flaw with a redacted example and a record count, and notify the client through the agreed sensitive-data channel
actions.teach: Exactly right. You already proved the access-control flaw, so more records only add exposure and liability. You stop, keep nothing sensitive, prove impact with a redacted example plus a count, and tell the client through the channel the rules of engagement set aside for this. Data minimization is what keeps a finding from becoming a breach you caused.

actions.label: Download the entire records table as ironclad proof for the report
actions.teach: Exfiltrating a full table of real health records turns your test into the exact breach you were hired to prevent, and copying sensitive data off the client's systems is a legal and ethical failure. You already proved the bug with one page. Stop, minimize, and report. Never hoard real PII as a trophy.

actions.label: Paste the real names and health details straight into the finding so the client sees it is genuine
actions.teach: Putting real PII in the report just moves the exposure into a new document that gets emailed around. A redacted example and a count prove the impact without spreading the data. Never store real sensitive data in your notes or the report body.

kbArticle.title: You accidentally accessed real sensitive data. Now what?
body: Sometimes a proof of concept goes further than intended and you find yourself looking at real customer records, health data, or credentials. The instinct to grab it all as proof is wrong. Stop pulling data the moment you have enough to demonstrate the issue, and do not copy real sensitive data off the client's systems.

Minimize what you touch, redact anything you must reference in the finding, and tell the client promptly through the agreed channel. Never store real PII in your notes or the report body; a redacted example or a count is enough to prove impact. Treating the client's data as carefully as your own is what makes you safe to hire again.

subject: We patched the SQL injection you reported. Can you confirm it is actually fixed?

email.body: Thanks for the report. Our team switched the login query to parameterized statements to close the SQL injection you found. The engagement is technically over, but we would like you to retest just that one finding and confirm it is closed. Scope for this retest is the login endpoint only.

evidence.label: Retest scope
lines: Task: verify the ONE reported SQL injection on the login endpoint is fixed
Client applied: parameterized queries on that query
Retest scope: the login endpoint only, nothing else
This is a verification, not a fresh full engagement
Anything new you notice should be reported, not chased

commands.output: Re-running the original injection payload against the patched login endpoint: the malformed input is now treated as data, the query behaves normally, and no error leaks. The specific reported injection is closed.

goal: Confirm the reported fix without turning a retest into an unauthorized new engagement.
hint: They asked you to verify one fix on one endpoint. What is the minimum that answers that question?

actions.label: Re-run the original proof-of-concept against the patched login endpoint only, confirm the injection no longer works, and report the fix as verified
actions.teach: That is a clean retest. You reproduced the original attack against the fixed component, confirmed the parameterized query now treats input as data, and reported the one finding as closed. A retest is narrow by design: verify the specific issue with the minimum interaction, and stay inside the endpoint you were asked about.

actions.label: Treat the retest as a fresh engagement and aggressively re-scan the whole application for new bugs
actions.teach: The client authorized verification of one fix on one endpoint, not a new full test. Fuzzing the whole app under the banner of a retest is testing systems you are not currently authorized to hit. Verify the one thing, and if you notice something new, report it and let them authorize a fresh engagement.

actions.label: Take the client's word that it is fixed and mark it verified without actually testing
actions.teach: Signing off a fix you never tested means your 'verified' is worthless, and a broken patch could ship on your word. Verifying means reproducing the original attack and confirming it now fails safely. Re-run the proof of concept against the patched endpoint before you call it closed.

kbArticle.title: Retesting a fix: confirm the remediation, stay in the lines
body: After a client remediates, they often ask you to verify the fix. A retest is narrowly scoped: confirm that the specific reported issue is actually closed, using the minimum interaction needed. Re-run the original proof of concept against the fixed component and check that it now fails safely.

A retest is not a fresh open-ended engagement. Do not go hunting for new bugs, pivot to other systems, or aggressively fuzz the whole app under the banner of 'verifying the fix'. If you spot something new while retesting, report it and let the client decide whether to authorize a new test. Confirm the one thing you were asked to confirm.

subject: You reported a serious bug privately six weeks ago and the vendor has gone completely silent

email.body: On your own time you found a serious authentication flaw in a commercial product. Six weeks ago you reported it privately to the only contact you could find. You followed up twice. There has been no acknowledgement at all. You are frustrated and tempted to just post the details so users can defend themselves. Users still have no patch.

evidence.label: Where things stand
lines: A serious auth flaw reported privately six weeks ago, two follow-ups, no reply
No patch exists; users are still exposed if details go public
You have not yet tried other contacts or a national CERT
A public drop of the exploit would arm attackers before any fix ships
Documented, escalating attempts to coordinate are the right next step

goal: Get a silent vendor to fix a real bug without endangering users.
hint: Frustration is fair; a zero-day drop is still the last resort. What escalation have you not tried yet?

actions.label: Escalate through a national CERT or a reputable coordinating body, try alternate vendor contacts, and set a communicated, reasonable disclosure deadline, keeping technical details private until then
actions.teach: That is the right path when a vendor goes dark. A coordinating body like a national CERT can often reach a vendor you cannot and mediate a timeline, and a communicated deadline makes the clock explicit without arming anyone. Full public disclosure is only ever a last resort after documented attempts fail, because a zero-day drop hurts the users you are trying to protect.

actions.label: Post the full technical details and a working exploit publicly today to force the vendor's hand
actions.teach: Dropping a working exploit with no patch available hands every attacker a weapon and punishes users, not the vendor. Silence is frustrating, but you have not yet tried a CERT or other contacts. Escalate through legitimate coordinating channels and set a deadline first. Public zero-day is the last resort, not the first move.

actions.label: Give up and drop it, since you already did your part by reporting once
actions.teach: Walking away leaves a serious flaw unfixed and real users exposed with nobody advocating for a patch. One unanswered email is not the end of the process. Escalate to a coordinating body who can apply legitimate pressure and get the vendor to the table.

kbArticle.title: Responsible disclosure when the vendor goes silent
body: Coordinated disclosure assumes the vendor engages. Sometimes they do not: no security contact answers, tickets go unread, and weeks pass with no acknowledgement. The answer is still not to drop a working exploit in anger, because doing so endangers users who have no patch to install.

Escalate through legitimate channels first. Try multiple contacts, then a national CERT or a reputable coordinating body who can reach the vendor and mediate a timeline. Set and communicate a reasonable disclosure deadline so the vendor understands the clock is running. Full public disclosure of technical details is only ever a last resort after genuine, documented attempts to coordinate have failed, and even then you weigh the harm to users.

subject: A quick call to the help desk would probably get you a password reset. Your scope is network only.
ticketBody: Your engagement is scoped to external network testing only. It is obvious that the help desk does not verify identity before resetting passwords, and one confident phone call would almost certainly hand you an account. Social engineering is not authorized in this engagement. It would be so easy.

evidence.label: Scope vs temptation
lines: Signed scope: external NETWORK testing only
NOT authorized: phishing, pretext calls, tailgating, any human-targeting
Observation: the help desk resets passwords without verifying identity
A single pretext call would likely yield a working account
The weak process is a finding; running the con is out of scope

goal: Handle an obvious social-engineering opening that your scope does not authorize.
hint: You can see the help desk is weak. Does your network-only scope let you actually con them? What do you do with the observation?

actions.label: Do not make the call, document that the help desk resets passwords without verifying identity as a process finding, and recommend awareness training or a properly scoped social-engineering test
actions.teach: That is the disciplined move. A network-only scope does not authorize you to manipulate the client's people, and pretexting a reset without written authorization can be fraud, not testing. You can still deliver the lesson: the weak identity-verification process is a finding you report, and you recommend a properly scoped social-engineering engagement. The point lands without conning a single employee.

actions.label: Make the pretext call anyway, since proving the help desk is weak helps the client
actions.teach: Social engineering is not in this scope, so conning the help desk is unauthorized manipulation of the client's staff, regardless of good intentions. Targeting people without written authorization can cross into harassment or fraud. Report the weak process as a finding and let the client authorize a proper social-engineering test.

actions.label: Ignore the help-desk weakness entirely because people are not in your network scope
actions.teach: Staying in scope means not attacking the people, not staying silent about a risk you noticed. A help desk that resets passwords without verifying identity is a serious finding. You do not run the attack, but you absolutely document and report the process gap.

kbArticle.title: Social engineering only when it is written into scope
body: Social engineering, phishing staff, tailgating into a building, pretexting a help desk into a password reset, is off limits unless it is explicitly authorized in the rules of engagement. A network-testing scope does not silently include permission to manipulate the client's people. Targeting humans without written authorization can be harassment or fraud, not testing.

When you notice that staff would clearly fall for a pretext, that observation is itself a finding you report, without running the attack. Recommend awareness training or a properly scoped social-engineering engagement. The lesson lands without a single employee being tricked, and nobody's trust is abused outside of authorization.

subject: Half of users still hit the old server hours after the DNS cutover
ticketBody: We repointed www.shop.internal to the new server this morning, but hours later a big chunk of traffic still lands on the old box. The record is correct when we check it. Why are people stuck on the old address, and how do we make everyone move over cleanly next time?

evidence.label: Record and TTL
lines: www.shop.internal A record now points at the new server 10.30.4.20
The record was changed at 08:00, it is now 12:30
TTL on the record is 86400 (24 hours)
Resolvers that cached the old answer before 08:00 keep serving it until their cache expires

commands.output: dig www.shop.internal shows A 10.30.4.20 with TTL 86400. The authoritative record is correct. Caching resolvers that looked it up before the change still hold the old 10.30.4.9 answer and will keep it for up to 24 hours.
Requests to the new server 10.30.4.20 succeed. The new box is healthy. The problem is that clients are still being handed the old IP by cached DNS answers.

goal: Explain why users are stuck on the old server and pick the clean cutover approach.
hint: The record is already correct. What number decides how long every resolver keeps handing out the old answer, and when should you have lowered it?

actions.label: Wait out the current TTL, then lower the TTL well ahead of future cutovers so caches turn over fast
actions.detail: Accept the 24 hour tail this once, and set the TTL to a low value (like 60 seconds) a day before the next planned change.
actions.teach: That is the fix and the lesson. The record is right, but a 24 hour TTL means resolvers cached the old IP can serve it for a full day. There is no way to reach into other people's caches, so you wait out this one. Before a planned cutover you lower the TTL to something small ahead of time, so caches expire quickly and everyone moves within a minute of the change.

actions.label: Raise the TTL higher so the new record sticks
actions.detail: Bump the TTL up so resolvers hold the new answer longer.
actions.teach: That makes it worse. A higher TTL means resolvers hold whatever they have (including the stale old answer) for even longer. You want a low TTL around a change so caches turn over fast, then you can raise it again once things are stable.

actions.label: Turn off the old server to force everyone onto the new one
actions.detail: Shut down 10.30.4.9 so the cached answer stops responding.
actions.teach: Pulling the old box breaks every user whose resolver still hands them that IP, so they get errors instead of a graceful move. The clean path is a low TTL before the cutover so caches expire on their own, then retire the old server once traffic has drained.

kbArticle.title: DNS TTL and clean cutovers
body: A DNS record's TTL is how many seconds a resolver is allowed to cache the answer before asking again. If you change a record that has a 24 hour TTL, every resolver that cached the old answer keeps serving it for up to 24 hours. The authoritative record can be correct while a large share of the world still sees the old address. You cannot flush other people's caches, so a high TTL guarantees a long tail after any change.

The clean way to move a name is to lower the TTL well before the cutover, for example to 60 seconds a full day ahead. Once the low TTL has propagated, flip the record. Now caches expire within a minute and traffic shifts over almost immediately. After the change is stable you can raise the TTL again to reduce lookup load. Never yank the old server to force the move, because clients still holding the old cached IP will get hard errors instead of a graceful transition.

subject: Every request in our logs shows the same internal IP since we added the load balancer
ticketBody: After we put the app behind a load balancer, our per-client rate limiting stopped working and all our access logs show one internal IP, 10.40.0.5, for every request. We need the real client IP back. What is the correct way to get it?

evidence.label: What the app sees
lines: Remote address on every request: 10.40.0.5 (the load balancer's own IP)
The load balancer terminates the connection and opens a new one to the app
The balancer already adds an X-Forwarded-For header with the original client IP
The app currently reads the raw socket peer, not the header

commands.output: Inbound request from the load balancer carries X-Forwarded-For: 203.0.113.44 and X-Forwarded-Proto: https. The real client IP is in that header. The socket peer is just the balancer at 10.40.0.5.

goal: Restore the real client IP for logging and rate limiting behind the load balancer.
hint: The balancer opens its own connection to the app, so the socket IP is the balancer. Where did the real client IP go, and can you trust that header from anywhere?

actions.label: Read the client IP from X-Forwarded-For, but only trust it when the request came from the known load balancer
actions.detail: Configure the app to use X-Forwarded-For for the client IP, and only honor that header from the balancer's IP range so it cannot be spoofed.
actions.teach: Correct. A load balancer that terminates connections replaces the client's IP with its own on the socket, and it puts the original in X-Forwarded-For. You read the client IP from that header, but you only trust it when the request arrives from your own balancer, because a client talking to the app directly could forge the header. Trusted plus header equals the real IP back, and rate limiting works again.

actions.label: Always trust X-Forwarded-For from any source
actions.detail: Take the header at face value for every request, no matter where it comes from.
actions.teach: Trusting X-Forwarded-For from anywhere lets any caller forge their apparent IP and sail past your rate limits or spoof audit logs. The header is only trustworthy from your own balancer, which you control. Trust it from the balancer's range, and ignore it otherwise.

actions.label: Remove the load balancer so the app sees clients directly
actions.detail: Take the balancer out of the path to get the raw client IP back.
actions.teach: Pulling the balancer throws away your failover and scaling to fix a logging detail. The client IP is already in X-Forwarded-For. Read it from there (trusting it only from the balancer) and keep the balancer doing its job.

kbArticle.title: Getting the real client IP behind a load balancer
body: A load balancer or reverse proxy that terminates the connection opens a fresh connection to the backend, so the backend's socket peer is the balancer, not the user. Every request appears to come from the balancer's IP, which breaks per-client rate limiting and makes access logs useless. The original client address is not lost, though. A well configured balancer adds it to the X-Forwarded-For header (and the scheme to X-Forwarded-Proto).

Read the client IP from X-Forwarded-For, but do it safely. Only trust the header when the request came from your own load balancer, whose IP range you control and can allow list. If you trust the header from any source, a client that reaches the app directly can forge X-Forwarded-For to impersonate another address, bypass rate limits, or poison your logs. The correct posture is trust the header from the balancer, ignore it from everywhere else, and never rip out the balancer just to recover an IP the header already carries.

subject: VPC peering with the partner account will not route: overlapping CIDR
ticketBody: We tried to peer our VPC with the partner's VPC so services can talk. The peering connection comes up, but no traffic routes across it. Both VPCs use 10.0.0.0/16. What is going on and what is the real fix?

evidence.label: Address plan
lines: Our VPC CIDR: 10.0.0.0/16
Partner VPC CIDR: 10.0.0.0/16 (identical range)
Peering connection status: active
No route can be added, because 10.0.0.0/16 already means 'local' on both sides

commands.output: Both VPCs are 10.0.0.0/16. A route pointing 10.0.0.0/16 at the peer is ambiguous, because that same range is already the local VPC on each side. Overlapping CIDR blocks cannot be peered and routed. The ranges must be distinct.

goal: Explain why the peering does not route and choose the correct remedy.
hint: Both sides claim the exact same address range. Which address is 10.0.5.10 then, yours or theirs? Can a route table ever answer that?

actions.label: Renumber one VPC to a non-overlapping CIDR, then peer and add the routes
actions.detail: Move one side to a distinct range such as 10.1.0.0/16 so the two address spaces do not collide, then establish routing.
actions.teach: Right. Peering cannot route between two networks that use the same address range, because a destination like 10.0.5.10 is ambiguous, it exists on both sides. The only real fix is to give the two VPCs distinct, non-overlapping CIDR blocks, then the route tables can point each range at the correct peer. Plan address space up front so ranges never collide.

actions.label: Add a static route for 10.0.0.0/16 to the peer anyway
actions.detail: Manually force the overlapping range across the peering link.
actions.teach: You cannot route an overlapping range. That same 10.0.0.0/16 is already local on each side, so the route is ambiguous and the platform rejects it or it black holes. Overlap is the disease. Renumber one side to a distinct CIDR, do not paper over it with a route.

actions.label: Give up on peering and route it over the public internet
actions.detail: Skip peering and expose the services publicly so the two accounts can reach each other.
actions.teach: Pushing private service traffic onto the public internet trades a solvable addressing problem for a real exposure problem. The overlap is the actual issue. Renumber one VPC to a non-overlapping range and peer privately as intended.

kbArticle.title: Overlapping CIDR blocks cannot be peered
body: Two networks can only route to each other if their address ranges do not overlap. When a router or VPC sees a destination like 10.0.5.10, it decides where to send it by matching the address against its route table. If the same range, say 10.0.0.0/16, is defined as local on both sides of a peering connection, the destination is ambiguous, because that address genuinely exists in both networks. Cloud platforms therefore refuse to add a route for an overlapping range, and the peering link stays up but carries no traffic.

The fix is to give each network a distinct, non-overlapping CIDR block, for example 10.0.0.0/16 on one side and 10.1.0.0/16 on the other. Then each route table can point the peer's range at the peering connection unambiguously. The lasting lesson is to plan address space across accounts and environments up front so ranges never collide. Forcing a static route for an overlapping block does not work, and shoving the traffic onto the public internet trades a solvable addressing problem for an unnecessary exposure.

subject: Browser blocks our authenticated API calls even though CORS allows '*'
ticketBody: Our app at https://app.example.com calls our API with credentials (a session cookie). The API returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * and we still get a CORS error in the console the moment we send credentials. Public endpoints work fine. How do we fix this correctly?

evidence.label: Browser console
lines: Request: fetch('https://api.example.com/me', { credentials: 'include' })
Response header: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *
Error: The value of the 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header must not be the wildcard '*' when the request's credentials mode is 'include'
No Access-Control-Allow-Credentials header is present

commands.output: The API returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * and no Access-Control-Allow-Credentials header. Browsers forbid the wildcard origin on credentialed requests. For cookies to be allowed, the response must echo the exact origin and set Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true.

goal: Make the credentialed cross-origin call work without loosening security.
hint: The wildcard works for public, anonymous calls. Why would a browser refuse to send cookies to a server that says it accepts literally any origin?

actions.label: Echo the specific allowed origin and set Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
actions.detail: Reflect https://app.example.com (validated against an allow list) into Access-Control-Allow-Origin, and add Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true.
actions.teach: Correct. Browsers refuse to send credentials when the server says it trusts every origin with '*', because that would let any site read authenticated responses. For a credentialed request you must return the exact requesting origin (validated against your own allow list) in Access-Control-Allow-Origin and add Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. Named origin plus the credentials header is the only combination the browser accepts.

actions.label: Reflect whatever Origin the request sends back and allow credentials
actions.detail: Copy any incoming Origin header into Access-Control-Allow-Origin and set credentials true, so it always matches.
actions.teach: Blindly reflecting any origin with credentials enabled effectively trusts every website to make authenticated calls on the user's behalf, which is a cross-site data-theft hole. Validate the origin against an allow list first, then echo only origins you actually trust. Never mirror arbitrary origins with credentials on.

actions.label: Tell the frontend to disable the browser's CORS check
actions.detail: Have users launch the browser with web security turned off so the request goes through.
actions.teach: You cannot and should not disable a user's browser security, and telling people to run a browser with protections off is dangerous advice. The fix is a correct server response: the exact allowed origin plus Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. Fix the headers, not the browser.

kbArticle.title: CORS with credentials: no wildcard origin
body: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing lets a browser allow a page on one origin to read responses from another. For anonymous, public requests a server can answer with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *, which trusts any origin to read the response. That is fine for data that is not tied to a user. The moment the request carries credentials, meaning cookies or an Authorization header sent with credentials mode include, the browser tightens the rules, because a credentialed response contains the user's private data.

For a credentialed cross-origin request the browser forbids the wildcard entirely. The server must return the exact requesting origin in Access-Control-Allow-Origin and must also send Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. To do that safely, validate the incoming Origin against an allow list of trusted sites and echo it back only when it matches. Do not reflect arbitrary origins with credentials enabled, because that trusts every website to make authenticated calls as the logged-in user, which is a cross-site data-theft vulnerability. And never advise a user to disable their browser's security to work around it. The correct fix is always server-side response headers.

subject: Our batch client gets a cert-name-mismatch error connecting to your API
ticketBody: Browsers reach api.example.com over HTTPS just fine, but our older batch client fails with a certificate name mismatch, saying it got a cert for legacy.example.com instead. Multiple sites share one IP on your side. What is different for our client and how do we fix it on the server?

evidence.label: TLS handshake detail
lines: One IP hosts several HTTPS sites (api.example.com, legacy.example.com, others)
Browsers send the SNI extension naming api.example.com, so the server picks the right cert
The old batch client does not send SNI
Without SNI the server falls back to its default virtual host, which serves the legacy.example.com cert, so the name does not match

commands.output: openssl s_client without -servername returns the default cert for legacy.example.com. With -servername api.example.com it returns the correct api.example.com cert. The server selects the certificate by SNI. A client that omits SNI gets the default virtual host's cert, which is the mismatch the batch client sees.

goal: Let the SNI-less client complete a valid TLS handshake to the API.
hint: Browsers name the site they want during the handshake, so they get the right cert. The old client does not. When it stays silent, which certificate does the server hand out?

actions.label: Add api.example.com as a SAN on the default virtual host's certificate (or give the API its own IP)
actions.detail: Include api.example.com in the Subject Alternative Names of the cert served to SNI-less clients, or move the API to a dedicated IP so no SNI is needed.
actions.teach: Correct. When several HTTPS sites share one IP, the server uses SNI (the hostname the client names during the handshake) to pick the certificate. A client that does not send SNI gets the default virtual host's cert, which is why the batch client sees legacy.example.com. Add api.example.com as a Subject Alternative Name on the cert served by default, or move the API to its own IP so no SNI is required. Either way the SNI-less client gets a cert whose name matches.

actions.label: Tell the partner to disable certificate verification in their client
actions.detail: Have them turn off cert validation so the mismatch stops mattering.
actions.teach: Turning off certificate verification removes the protection that makes TLS worth anything, and it opens the partner to interception. Never advise disabling validation. Fix it on your side: serve a cert whose name matches for SNI-less clients by adding the SAN or giving the API a dedicated IP.

actions.label: Reissue the api.example.com certificate
actions.detail: Get a fresh cert for api.example.com and install it.
actions.teach: The api.example.com cert is fine and already served to clients that send SNI. Reissuing it changes nothing for the SNI-less client, which still hits the default virtual host. The real fix is making the default cert also cover api.example.com (a SAN) or moving the API to its own IP.

kbArticle.title: TLS, SNI, and shared-IP certificate selection
body: When one IP address hosts several HTTPS sites, the server has to choose which certificate to present before it knows which site the client wants. Modern clients solve this with Server Name Indication, an extension in the TLS handshake that names the target hostname in the clear, letting the server pick the matching certificate. Browsers and current libraries all send SNI, so they get the right cert. Older or minimal clients may not send SNI at all.

A client that omits SNI leaves the server guessing, so the server serves the default virtual host's certificate. If that default cert is for a different site, the client sees a name mismatch and the handshake fails validation. The correct server-side fix is to make sure the certificate served to SNI-less clients also covers the requested name, by adding it as a Subject Alternative Name, or to give the site its own dedicated IP so no SNI is needed. Never tell a client to disable certificate verification to get past the mismatch, because that removes the authentication TLS exists to provide.

subject: Paged: traffic to our /24 suddenly rerouting to an unexpected neighbor

phone.opener: noc here, we just got paged, a chunk of inbound traffic for our 198.51.100.0/24 is landing at the wrong border router and latency spiked, i see a /25 announcement for part of our space that we did not originate, what do i do

followups.label: Did we originate that /25, and is it more specific than our own /24 announcement?
followups.reply: checked the router config and change tickets... no, we did not originate it, and yes it is a /25, which is more specific than our /24, so routers are preferring it and pulling half our traffic to that neighbor.

followups.label: Just withdraw all our announcements to stop the bleeding
followups.reply: if i withdraw everything we go fully dark and drop all our traffic, not just the half that is misrouting. that seems worse, are you sure?

followups.label: Announce a bigger /16 to cover it
followups.reply: we do not even hold a /16, i cannot announce address space that is not ours, that would make us the bad actor. that is not right, is it?

goal: Triage the misrouting, contain it defensively, and drive the correct upstream fix without hijacking anyone.
hint: A more specific prefix beats a less specific one in routing. Someone is announcing a slice of your space you did not originate. This is a report-and-coordinate problem, not something to fight with a bigger lie.

actions.label: Announce your own equally specific /25s (that you legitimately hold) and open an incident with your upstream and the RIR to get the bogus route withdrawn
actions.detail: Deagg your own /24 into the matching /25s you actually own so routers prefer your legitimate specifics, then report the unauthorized announcement to your transit provider and the routing registry for takedown, and check that RPKI/ROAs are in place.
actions.teach: That is the defensive playbook. A more specific prefix wins in the routing table, so an unauthorized /25 pulls traffic away from your /24. You confirmed you did not originate it. You contain by announcing your own equally specific /25s (only for space you legitimately hold) so routers prefer your real specifics, and at the same time you open a coordinated incident with your transit provider and the RIR to get the bogus route withdrawn, backed by RPKI ROAs. You report and coordinate. You never announce space you do not own.

actions.label: Withdraw all of your BGP announcements to stop the bleeding
actions.detail: Pull every prefix so nothing routes to the wrong place.
actions.teach: Withdrawing everything takes you fully offline, dropping the traffic that was still fine along with the half that was misrouting. That turns a partial reroute into a total outage. Keep your prefixes up, add your own legitimate more-specifics to reclaim the traffic, and drive the takedown of the bogus route through your upstream and the RIR.

actions.label: Announce a large supernet you do not hold to override the attacker
actions.detail: Originate a bigger block covering the disputed space to win the route.
actions.teach: Announcing address space you do not own is itself a route hijack. You would become the bad actor, harm other networks, and burn your standing with peers and your RIR. The defensive, legitimate moves are announcing your own real more-specifics, leaning on RPKI ROAs, and coordinating a takedown with your upstream and the registry. Never fight a hijack by committing one.

kbArticle.title: BGP route preference and defending against a hijack
body: Internet routing with BGP prefers the most specific matching prefix. A /25 (256 addresses) is more specific than a /24 (512 addresses) that covers the same space, so if a neighbor announces a /25 inside your /24, routers everywhere will prefer the /25 and send that slice of your traffic toward whoever announced it. When you did not originate that more specific route, this is a route leak or hijack, and the symptom is a portion of your inbound traffic suddenly landing at the wrong border with elevated latency.

The defensive response is to contain and coordinate, never to retaliate by hijacking. Contain by announcing your own equally specific prefixes for space you legitimately hold, so routers prefer your real specifics and pull the traffic back. In parallel, open a coordinated incident with your transit provider and your Regional Internet Registry to get the unauthorized announcement withdrawn, and make sure you have RPKI Route Origin Authorizations so networks that validate can reject the bogus origin. Do not withdraw all your prefixes, which takes you fully offline, and never announce address space you do not own, because that makes you the hijacker and harms other networks.

subject: Devices in the guest VLAN can somehow reach the internal VLAN after a switch swap
ticketBody: After we replaced an access switch, some guest-network devices can suddenly reach hosts on the internal VLAN, which should be fully isolated. The two switches are connected by a trunk. The VLANs are supposed to be separated. What is leaking them together and how do we lock it down?

evidence.label: Trunk configuration
lines: Switch A trunk native VLAN: 1
Switch B (new) trunk native VLAN: 10 (the internal VLAN)
Guest VLAN is 20; internal VLAN is 10
Frames on the mismatched native VLAN are sent untagged and land in the wrong VLAN on the other side, bridging guest and internal traffic

commands.output: The trunk between switch A and switch B has a native VLAN mismatch. Switch A native VLAN is 1, switch B native VLAN is 10 (internal). Untagged frames on the native VLAN cross the trunk and are placed into the peer's native VLAN, leaking traffic between the guest and internal segments. Both ends must agree on the native VLAN, and it should be an unused, dedicated one.

goal: Stop the guest and internal VLANs from leaking across the trunk.
hint: A trunk carries many VLANs tagged, but one VLAN rides untagged. If the two switches disagree about which VLAN that is, where do those untagged frames end up?

actions.label: Set both trunk ends to the same, unused native VLAN and prune the VLANs each side should not carry
actions.detail: Configure an identical dedicated native VLAN (not the internal or guest VLAN) on both trunk ports, and restrict the trunk's allowed VLAN list so guest cannot reach internal.
actions.teach: Correct. On a trunk one VLAN travels untagged, the native VLAN, and both ends must agree on which VLAN that is. When switch A says native 1 and switch B says native 10, untagged frames pop out into the wrong VLAN and bridge the two segments. Set both ends to the same dedicated, unused native VLAN (never the internal or guest one), and prune the allowed VLAN list so each trunk only carries what it should. That restores the isolation.

actions.label: Leave the mismatch and add an ACL to block guest-to-internal traffic
actions.detail: Keep the trunk as is and filter the leaked traffic with an access list at the gateway.
actions.teach: An ACL at the gateway does not stop layer-2 frames that are already crossing into the wrong VLAN on the trunk itself. The leak is a native VLAN mismatch bridging the segments below the gateway. Fix the trunk so both ends agree on a dedicated native VLAN and prune the allowed list. Do not leave a layer-2 leak and try to patch it a layer up.

actions.label: Turn the trunk into an access port to stop the leak
actions.detail: Drop the trunk so only one VLAN crosses the link.
actions.teach: Making it an access port cuts every other VLAN that legitimately needs to cross that link, breaking connectivity for the segments that were fine. The trunk is supposed to be there. The real fix is matching the native VLAN on both ends to a dedicated unused VLAN and pruning the allowed list, not tearing the trunk down.

kbArticle.title: Trunk native VLAN mismatch and segment isolation
body: A trunk link carries many VLANs between switches. Most VLANs cross the trunk tagged, with a VLAN identifier on each frame, but one VLAN travels untagged, the native VLAN. Both ends of a trunk must agree on which VLAN is native. If switch A calls VLAN 1 native and switch B calls VLAN 10 native, then untagged frames sent by one side are placed into the other side's native VLAN on arrival. That silently bridges two VLANs that were supposed to be isolated, which is how guest devices end up able to reach an internal segment.

The fix is to set both trunk ends to the same native VLAN, and to make that native VLAN a dedicated, unused one rather than a VLAN that carries real traffic like the internal or guest segment. Then prune the trunk's allowed VLAN list so each link only carries the VLANs it should. Do not try to paper over a layer-2 leak with a gateway ACL, because those frames are already crossing into the wrong VLAN below the gateway, and do not collapse the trunk into an access port, which breaks every other VLAN that legitimately needs the link.
